t1.1.text:
The Eques didn't talk on the drive.

He wasn't unkind about it. He just had a face that didn't seem to need talking. The transport was an electric SUV, almost silent on the road, and you could hear the camera at the wall checkpoint clicking through its routine — flash, beep, plate logged, two faces cross-referenced, gate up — without anyone in the car saying a word. We took the elevated highway into the Capitol, past the second checkpoint, past the wall I'd only ever seen from the outside. The city changed under the wheels.

The streets I knew were narrow and warm and smelled like frying oil. The streets we drove now were wide and clean and had no smell at all. Every intersection had a surveillance pole. Every pole had a camera, and a microphone, and the small white pyramid I'd read once on a forum — before the forum was taken down — was the AI module that listened for the wrong words.

I kept thinking he would say something. *"You'll get used to it."* Or *"Don't worry."* Or *"Your friend is fine."* He didn't. He didn't even glance at me. I suppose if you do this for a living you stop looking at the cargo.

The Thebes facility was on the eastern slope of the Capitol — three low concrete buildings that had been a public high school once, before the Council had needed the campus more than the kids had needed an education. You could still see the painted line for a basketball court in the courtyard, faded almost to nothing under the new security striping. Four other recruits were standing around with duffel bags. None of them looked like volunteers.

The Eques handed me to a clerk who scanned my thumb into a tablet, printed a temporary badge on a lanyard, and handed me a uniform that was a size too big. He said, "Lights at twenty-two-hundred. Up at five. Don't try to leave the perimeter." Then he looked past me to the next one.

I sat on my bunk. I held the uniform in my lap. I watched the small red light above the bunk-room door — a camera, the same model as in the cars I'd never been able to afford — and I tried not to think about Quinn.

There was a window. Outside the window, very far away, the lights of my old neighborhood were just visible past the wall.

I told myself I was here to fix it. I told myself I had a plan.

I was fifteen. I didn't have a plan.

t1.2.text:
Morning. I had an hour before training. The facility was quiet — most of the recruits were already badged into assignments, doing the kind of work nobody admits Eques do. Hauling pallets at the central logistics yard. Mopping floors in administrative buildings. Standing very still in lobbies. The same labor any kid in any Quadrant did, but with a uniform on and a scannable badge, which apparently made it different.

An hour. I could feel the hour like a weight in my hand.

I knew what I should do. The thing I'd promised myself in the car, watching the city change. The reason I'd told myself I was here. *Find Quinn. Help him. Get information.*

I also knew Bran was about a forty-minute walk away, sitting at our kitchen table doing schoolwork, alone, because nobody had told him anything. The cameras between here and there would log my route, but my new badge said I had limited liberty.

And the senior recruits had been very specific in the briefing about how the smartest thing a new Thebes could do was *put his head down and work*.

Three things. One hour. I had to choose.

choices.label: Try to find out what happened to Quinn.

t1.3a.text:
I changed out of the uniform in a public toilet two blocks past the facility checkpoint. Civilian clothes. Old jacket. Hood up against the cameras at the corner of every block. I felt absurd. I'd been an Eques for fourteen hours.

The walk home was the strangest thing I'd done in my life. Same streets. Same cracked tile in front of the bakery. Same dog at the corner. Same surveillance dome on the lamppost outside the bus stop. None of it knew I'd left.

Bran was at the kitchen table doing his fractions on a screen. He looked up. His face did the small bright thing it does, and then it did a smaller, more careful thing, because he is twelve and he's already learned that bright is dangerous.

He said, "I thought you were at your special program."

I sat down across from him. He pushed half his rice over to my side of the plate without looking at it.

I tried to figure out what I could say.

choices.label: "Tell him the truth — he can keep a secret."

t1.3a_truth.text:
I told him.

Not all of it. Not Quinn's file. Not the Eques's voice in the hallway. But the shape of it — that I was at the Thebes facility, that I hadn't chosen to be there, that he could not say a word to anyone — not at school, not to Mom, *especially* not to Mom, and not on his phone, not even by text, not even joking — until I told him otherwise.

He listened the way he listens when he's deciding whether something is real.

Then he said, "Okay."

That was all. *Okay.*

I almost broke open right there. Twelve years old. *Okay.*

I was back at the facility before training. Bran had eaten the rest of the rice himself.

t1.3a_lie.text:
I told him I was fine. I told him the program was hard but the food was good, which was a lie, and that I'd be home for visits, which I didn't know if was a lie. I told him to keep doing his fractions.

I watched myself doing it. The way you watch a stranger order something at a restaurant.

Bran nodded along. He didn't believe me. He knew the rule about bright things.

But he wanted to. And so he let me.

I left the apartment lighter than I came in. Which was the worst part — that lying to him made it easier to walk back past the cameras to the facility.

t1.3a_silent.text:
I didn't try to explain. I just sat with him.

He seemed to understand that explanations weren't on offer. He kept doing his fractions. I corrected one of them, the way I always had. He laughed at me a little for being a know-it-all, the way he always had.

For about ten minutes, in the middle of the worst week of my life, I was a regular older brother in a regular kitchen with a regular Tuesday afternoon.

Then I left.

He didn't ask when I'd be back. I didn't tell him.

t1.3b.text:
The Skiritai records corridor was on the second floor. Thebes weren't allowed up there. The reasonable thing to do, on my first morning, was not to test that.

I was not feeling reasonable.

I walked up the stairs like I belonged. The biometric door at the top read someone else's badge — a Skiritai who'd left it active going in — and I slipped in behind her on the same scan. Nobody stopped me. Nobody was looking. I'd already noticed that most of the Eques *moved* like they were watching everything, but they weren't actually watching. They were performing a posture. There's a difference.

The records room had a glass door with a viewing window. Inside, two Skiritai stood at a workstation under three vertical monitors, talking about a case. I caught the word *file*. I caught the word *kid*. I caught the word *Quadrant*.

I caught — I am almost sure — the word *Quinn*.

I had to choose how close to get.

choices.label: Risk being seen — get closer to hear more.

t1.3b_close.text:
I pressed against the doorframe. I could hear better. I could hear *almost* enough.

— *can't prove the intrusion was him —*
— *option B then —*
— *just clean it, scrub the metadata, close the file —*

One of them turned toward the door.

I moved like I'd been walking past, head down, holding a clipboard I'd grabbed off a passing cart for exactly this reason. He looked at me through the glass. He had grey eyes and a small scar on his lip and he was filing my face away in whatever facial-recognition database lived behind his own eyes — and probably the one on the wall behind him, too.

I kept walking. I made it to the stairs. My hands didn't shake until I was back in the bunkroom.

I'd learned almost nothing. I'd been seen.

I told myself it was worth it. *Almost* enough was something.

t1.3b_safe.text:
I stayed back. I caught fragments. *Can't prove. Option B. Scrub it.*

I learned almost nothing.

I told myself that almost nothing was better than something I would have paid for. It was the kind of sentence I would say to myself a lot, in the next four years.

t1.3c.text:
The gym was in the basement. Concrete walls, LED-strip lights, the smell of every gym that has ever been, and a wall-mounted screen looping body-mechanics videos with the sound off. Two senior recruits were on the bench when I came in. They watched.

I did the warmups. I did them well. I had been a runner, before — the kind of skinny kid who could outpace bigger boys on a long enough course — and the body was still there, somewhere under the panic.

The senior at the door watched me through the whole thing. I didn't look at him. He had hard pale eyes and a burn scar that started under his jaw and went down into his collar. He was older than the recruits. The badge clipped to his collar had a small gold border I hadn't seen on anyone else's. I didn't know his name yet. I would, eventually.

He came over after I'd finished. He said, "You're new."

I said, "Yes, sir."

He said, "You're good. Don't be louder about it than you have to be."

He waited a beat. He wanted me to say something.

t1.3c_thank.text:
I said *thank you, sir*, the way I'd been raised to say it. I meant it.

He nodded once and turned away.

I felt a thing I had not expected to feel. *Pride.* The hot, specific pride of being told you're good at the thing you didn't want to be good at.

I went back to the warmup mat and did another set, and I could not tell, by the time I was done, whether I was working that hard for myself or for the man with the scar.

I am not sure I knew the answer to that question, ever again.

t1.3c_brush.text:
I shrugged. I said, *yeah, I guess.*

He looked at me a second too long. Then he walked back to the bench.

It was the right move, I think. I had decided, on the drive in, that I was not going to be one of *those* recruits — the ones who wanted to be liked. The ones who got promoted because they smiled.

I did one more set of warmups. The man with the scar didn't watch me anymore. The ceiling camera tracking me did.

I felt nothing. That was good. *Nothing* was the goal.

t1.4.text:
Lunch was rice and a grey thing that was probably meat. The mess hall was loud. I was already learning that the loudness was a courtesy — the louder it was, the harder the audio-analytics on the ceiling mics had to work.

A boy sat down across from me. He hadn't asked.

"Hi," he said. "You're the kid from yesterday."

I looked at him. Average height. Dark skin. Tight twists. Tired hazel eyes that weren't sleepy — they were *worn*, the way old furniture is worn. He was maybe my age. Maybe a year older. He was eating slowly and watching the room without seeming to.

"I'm Kaden," he said. "Don't worry, I'm not friendly."

"Okay."

"I just wanted to see you up close. Word is you're smart. Word is they know you're smart."

I said, "Word is whatever, I guess."

He smiled, a little. The smile didn't reach the rest of his face. The rest of his face didn't move.

"Listen," he said. "I'm not going to give you the speech. I'm going to give you one sentence. The mics don't just listen for words — they pattern-match cadence, vocabulary, how often you pause, who you sit next to twice in a row. If you ask the wrong questions, they'll flag you. If they flag you, you're done. Whatever you came here to fix — eat lunch first. You can fix it slower. It's allowed."

He went back to eating.

I sat there with my fork in my hand.

choices.label: Trust him a little — say more.

t1.4_trust.text:
I said, "Why are you telling me this?"

He looked up. The tired eyes did a thing. Almost amused.

"Because you remind me of me. Six months ago. And I'd have done a lot for someone to sit down and tell me to eat lunch first."

He didn't say anything else. He finished his rice. He took his tray and left.

I ate my lunch. Slowly. He had been right about that part.

t1.4_guarded.text:
I said, "Thanks for the advice."

He nodded like that was the answer he expected.

He finished his rice. He took his tray. He left.

I ate my lunch. Slowly. Because he had been right, and because acknowledging it would have cost me something I wasn't sure I had to spend.

t1.4_mock.text:
I said, "Eat your rice, then. Some of us have plans."

He laughed. It was not a happy laugh. It was the laugh of somebody watching a slow car crash and being relieved that it wasn't his.

"Sure, hero," he said. "Plans."

He took his tray. He left.

I finished my lunch. Quickly. I told myself the speed was a discipline. It wasn't. It was that the room felt different now that he was gone, and not in a way I wanted to sit with.

t1.5.text:
The supervising Skiritai's name was Officer Vell. He was the kind of man whose hands looked clean even when they weren't.

He put a tablet in front of me. The form on the screen was pre-loaded: a recruit's name, a Quadrant ID, a small dropdown with twelve risk categories. The category at the top, already highlighted, said LOW COHESION.

The name was *Talus Hadrik.*

Talus had the bunk three down from mine. He'd told me that morning, while we were folding sheets, that his sister's wedding was next week and he was sad to miss it.

Vell put a stylus next to the tablet.

"Sign it," he said. "Standard intake. Every new Thebes does one of these in the first week. You ID someone you think might struggle. The system helps them."

I stared at the screen.

I knew what *help* meant in this building. The system did not help. The system *processed*.

Vell said, "It's a checkbox, Mika."

I had not told him my first name.

conditionalText.append: *He had grey eyes and a small scar on his lip. He'd seen me once already, that morning, walking past the records room with someone else's clipboard. He hadn't said anything about it. He didn't have to. His tablet probably already had me flagged.*

choices.label: Sign it, but type a note in the comments field.
Report the order to a senior Eques.

t1.5_refuse.text:
I said, "No."

I said it quietly. I said it like I'd been practicing.

Vell didn't react. He picked the tablet up. He locked the screen with his thumb. He looked at me for a long moment. Then he said, "Okay."

He left the room.

Two days later I was reassigned to the laundry. Three weeks later I was reassigned to facility-cleaning duty in the East Quadrant satellite office. Within a month I was off the Eques roster entirely — discharged for "failure to integrate," my biometric clearance downgraded to civilian.

They didn't kill me. That would have been a waste. They processed me into the uniformed labor pool in the East Quadrant, where I cleaned offices for the rest of my life. The cameras at every intersection knew me, but in the dull way the cameras knew everyone — not the specific way they had been about to know me.

I was alive.

Quinn was not.

Bran grew up. He never asked what happened. He understood there were things you didn't ask, things you certainly didn't search online.

Sometimes, late at night, I told myself that I had won. That the system hadn't gotten me. That refusing the form had kept me clean.

I knew that wasn't true.

t1.5_report.text:
I said, "I'd like to talk to your superior."

Vell smiled.

It was the only time I would ever see Vell smile, and the smile told me, before he said a word, that I had made a mistake of the kind you don't get to make twice. He was probably already messaging the senior on his tablet under the desk.

The senior Eques he took me to was already expecting me. The *report* I had wanted to file became, in his hands, a different kind of document — and the meeting room had no cameras, the kind of room you don't find on a floor plan.

I didn't make it through the night.

The story they told Bran was that I had died honorably in a training accident. The notification came as a text with a press-release attachment.

It was the most flattering lie they ever told about me.

t1.6.text:
I signed the form. Vell took the tablet. He didn't say anything else.

I sat at the desk after he left, alone, and I was suddenly fifteen years old in a different hallway, two weeks before. I had been walking past a half-open door at the public library — a building the Council had quietly bought and turned into a regional records substation, although they had not changed the sign on the outside. I had been looking through the door without meaning to. I had been seeing Quinn's face on a monitor. I had been seeing the case file open beside it — *Q. Reilan, intrusion attempt, pending* — and the Eques at the workstation turning his head and seeing me.

The boy I'd been, in that hallway, had thought there was still a chance the world made sense.

I was looking at him now from across two weeks and one signature.

He looked very small.

t1.7.text:
Six months passed. Then four more.

The work was not as bad as I'd feared. Some of it was real labor — moving crates at the central logistics yard, cleaning offices in administrative buildings, the kind of work my mother used to do. Some of it was forms. The forms were the hard part. Every few weeks, another tablet, another checkbox, another name. I got faster at it. I told myself this was strategy — that the faster I got, the less the system flagged me, and the higher I rose, the more I could fix.

I told myself a lot of things, that year.

The promotion came in the spring. Twenty of us in the courtyard, the basketball line still faded under our boots. They called my name. They handed me a black Skiritai uniform with silver shoulder insignia. They reissued my badge — same chip, new clearance — and they handed me a service pistol I didn't yet know how to use.

I was sixteen.

My mother had not been told. Bran did not know. Quinn was still in a holding facility I had not yet found.

I stood up straight. I let them put the insignia on. I looked, for a moment, at the senior who had watched me train. The one with the scar. He was at the back of the courtyard. He nodded, once, like he'd been expecting this for months.

I didn't know his name yet.

His name was Jason.

t2.1.text:
The Skiritai induction was held in a small plaza inside the Capitol wall, which I had now passed through so many times the biometric gate didn't even slow down for me anymore. Eighteen of us. Black uniforms. Silver insignia. A drone hovered overhead recording the ceremony for the Council's internal feed.

The girl beside me was laughing.

She was laughing at something a senior had said while he was pinning her insignia, and the laugh was loud and unselfconscious and it had the particular quality of a laugh that had never been made to be quiet. I had grown up in apartments where laughter was something you kept under a hand because the walls were thin and the building had been wired. This laughter belonged in rooms with high ceilings and no microphones.

She was tall. Pale. Long platinum hair in a high braid. The uniform fit her like it had been tailored — which, looking at the cut, it had been.

She caught me staring and didn't pretend not to notice. "You look terrified," she said.

"I'm fine."

"You look terrified, but in a polite way. I'm Sophie."

"Mika."

"Mika what?"

"Just Mika."

She looked at me a second too long, then smiled like she'd decided something. "Okay, Just Mika. My father's a Hetairoi, so I'm going to be the worst person you've ever met, and I'm telling you that up front so you don't have to figure it out."

I almost laughed.

It was the first time in a year that something had almost gotten a real laugh out of me.

choices.label: Match her warmth — make a friend.

t2.1_warm.text:
I let myself smile back. "I'll keep that in mind."

"You should. I'll be insufferable about it."

The senior moved down the line and reached me, pinned the insignia on my collar, said the thing they said. *Service is privilege. Privilege is service.* I didn't hear it. Sophie was muttering something under her breath about how the man's hairline had a comb-over she couldn't unsee.

I was going to have a friend in this place.

I had not been planning on that.

t2.1_distant.text:
"Good to know," I said. I gave her a small smile. The kind I'd been practicing on senior officers.

She blinked. The smile she'd been wearing didn't go anywhere — she kept it intact — but a different intelligence came in behind her eyes. The intelligence of a girl who had been raised to read rooms and was reading me right now and finding me interesting in a way she hadn't been before.

"Hm," she said.

She didn't try to talk to me again that day.

She would, later. But she'd taken a measurement, and the measurement would be there forever.

t2.2.text:
The Skiritai station was a different building than the Thebes facility — newer, glass-fronted, three stories of polished concrete and steel with the Council's geometric crest backlit above the entrance. The locker room had a window that looked out over a section of the Capitol I had never been able to see — high-end residential, retail under awnings, citizens who walked like the cameras weren't there because for them they weren't.

A wall in the duty room cycled live feeds from a hundred surveillance points across the Capitol. We were given a lot more access now, which meant a lot more rope.

I had three things I was thinking about.

Sophie was in the next row of lockers, telling somebody a story about a horse. I could spend more time with her. There was no harm in it. (*There was harm in it. I knew there was harm in it. I told myself there wasn't.*)

I had Skiritai-level access to the case management system now. Not full access — that was Achaean — but enough to surface Quinn's file if I was careful with the audit logs. I could spend an evening with my head in the records and learn something real.

And I hadn't seen Niko in a year. He was still in the old neighborhood. Last I'd heard, he was driving a delivery scooter for a bakery and posting opinions about the Council to small encrypted forums nobody had asked him to post on.

Three things. A month off the duty rotation. I had to choose.

choices.label: Use Skiritai access to investigate Quinn.
Risk visiting Niko in the old neighborhood.

t2.3a.text:
She was in the gym every morning at six. So was I. We had not planned this. It happened the way friendship happens between two people who are bad at admitting they want it.

We trained. We sparred. She was stronger than me and I was faster than her, and we landed in a rhythm where neither of us could win cleanly, and we both, for the first time in our lives, found it pleasant to lose to someone.

She told me about her family. Her father was on the Council — *the food and resources committee*, she said, like she was apologizing for it being that one and not a more glamorous one. Her mother had died when she was nine. She had three older brothers, all of them in uniformed services somewhere. Her father had wanted a fourth son. He'd gotten Sophie instead. *I think it's worked out for him*, she said.

She didn't ask about my family. Not for two weeks. When she finally did, she did it the right way — at the end of a sparring session, both of us flat on our backs on the mat, breathing hard, looking up at the camera in the corner of the ceiling.

"What's your family like, Just Mika?"

I had practiced an answer. I had several practiced answers. I had a whole rotation.

I had to choose which version of myself to give her.

choices.label: Give her the version she wants.

t2.3a_real.text:
I told her about Bran.

Not the rest of it — not Quinn, not the ultimatum, not the mother who didn't know I was an Eques and wouldn't be told until she was. But Bran I gave her. I told her about the fractions on the screen. I told her about the rice he pushed across the table. I told her about the way he'd learned that bright was dangerous.

She didn't say anything for a long time.

Then she said, "You should have him visit. The Eques have family weekends. They're horrible. Everyone pretends. But you should do it anyway."

I told her I'd think about it.

I knew I wouldn't. The system would learn his face, his gait, his thumbprint. Once he'd been logged into an Eques facility he was in the database forever.

But I'd given her something true, and she'd held it carefully, and she'd given me something back: the knowledge that she could hold things.

It was the kind of thing I would not have understood was valuable, a year before.

t2.3a_perform.text:
I told her my parents were dead.

They weren't. My mother was alive in a fourth-floor apartment in the East Quadrant, working two jobs, and she was, to my knowledge, still asking my brother every Sunday whether he had heard from me.

But the lie was easier than the explanation. It was also the lie a girl like Sophie expected. *Tragic backstory, scrappy origin, climbed his way up*, that whole register. She accepted it without question. She said she was sorry.

I said it was a long time ago. I let my voice do the small thing that voices do when they want to be left alone about a topic.

She let me.

I felt nothing. That was the goal.

I noticed I had now used the phrase *that was the goal* twice in my own head about my own behavior, in the last year, and that the second time it had been easier than the first.

t2.3b.text:
Skiritai access wasn't full access. I couldn't pull a sealed file or close one or move one. I could *read* most things, with restrictions, and I could read them after midnight, when the audit-log review was on a different shift's rotation and the duty officer was usually asleep at her workstation.

It took me four nights to find Quinn.

He was alive. He was in a low-security holding facility in the third Quadrant — a converted shipping warehouse the Council didn't list on any public-facing infrastructure map. His case had been *open and pending review* for fifteen months — meaning the Council had not decided what to do with him and was in no hurry. They were waiting to see if the underground he'd allegedly tried to help — a small group that had been routing data around the Council's mandatory ID gateways — showed any sign of being worth the leverage. If it didn't, they'd close his file. *Close* was a word I'd learned, in this place, had two meanings.

I read his physical description. He was thinner. They had shaved his head. The intake photo showed him without his glasses, which somehow felt worse than the head.

I had two options.

choices.label: Try to pass him a message.
Note the file's location, leave it alone, save it for later.

t2.3b_message.text:
I used an internal Eques courier line. I encrypted it badly on purpose — a low-grade encryption that would look like an ex-girlfriend's idiot kid brother had sent it, not a Skiritai. The message was three words.

*Still here. Holding.*

He would know who it was. He'd been the only person I'd ever told about my private notation system, the one I used for calculus homework. Three words in that notation, on a low-grade encryption layer, was unmistakably me.

It was also unmistakably *traceable*, if anyone in the Council's analytics pipeline cared to look. The workstation was logged to my badge. The keystroke timing was logged. The send was logged.

I told myself the risk was acceptable.

I told myself the risk was acceptable a lot, that year. The phrase had a kind of warmth to it. *I told myself the risk was acceptable* meant: *I am the kind of person who weighs risks now.*

t2.3b_note.text:
I memorized the file location. I closed the case viewer. I didn't even copy the case number — I trusted myself to remember it. I cleared my session log and walked out.

There was nothing I could do with Skiritai clearance. To pull him out of holding, or to close the file in a way that helped him, I'd need Achaean clearance. And Achaeans signed kill orders, which meant by the time I could help him, I would have done other things to get there.

I noted, lying in my bunk that night, that this trade was beginning to make sense to me.

I noted, also, that *making sense to me* was the kind of phrase a person uses when they are translating something terrible into a language they can use without flinching.

t2.3c.text:
Niko picked the alley. He did not invite me to the bakery and he did not come to the Capitol. He picked an alley three blocks from his apartment, at eleven at night — a blind spot between two camera arcs that I had checked on the Skiritai mapping tool before I'd agreed — and when he saw me coming he didn't smile.

He was taller than I remembered. Or I'd gotten shorter. Both, probably.

"You came."

"You said to come."

"I said it like a test. Some Eques wouldn't bother."

I didn't have an answer to that. He hadn't said it like a test. We both knew he hadn't said it like a test. He was performing the new shape of our friendship in real time and I was supposed to figure it out.

He said, "Where's Quinn?"

That was the whole conversation. He had not asked how I was. He had not asked about training. He had not asked, the way I had thought he might, whether I was okay. He'd asked one thing.

I had to choose what to say.

choices.label: Partial truth — Quinn's alive, that's all you know.
The whole truth — beg him to keep quiet.

t2.3c_lie.text:
"I don't know," I said. "I haven't heard. I can't ask. They watch us."

He looked at me for a long time.

"Mika," he said. "When did you get good at lying?"

I didn't answer.

He nodded slowly, like the answer to that question was the answer to a different question, and the different question was the only one he'd really come to ask.

He said, "Get out of my city."

He left first. I waited five minutes, then walked back, varying my route to avoid the camera I'd entered through.

I was already composing, in my head, the next three lies I would have to tell him if he ever spoke to me again.

I noticed, walking, that I was good at it. I noticed I felt nothing about being good at it. I noticed that *noticing nothing* had become something I noticed.

t2.3c_partial.text:
"He's alive," I said. "Last I knew. That's all I have."

"Where?"

"I can't say."

"Can't, or won't?"

"Both."

He waited. He wanted more. I didn't have more to give him without giving him too much. I held his eye and let the silence do what silence does between people who have known each other a long time, which is, sometimes, more honest than words.

He nodded, finally. "Okay."

He said it the way Bran had said it, a year before, at the kitchen table. *Okay.* The same word. The same disappointment, dressed in patience.

He left.

I noticed, as he walked away, that my hands were not shaking. I would have expected my hands to be shaking.

That was new.

t2.3c_truth.text:
I told him.

All of it. The library hallway. The file. The Eques who caught me. The ultimatum. The facility. The morale report on a tablet. Quinn alive in a third-Quadrant warehouse, head shaved, file open and pending. The whole thing in twelve minutes, in a camera blind spot, while a stray cat watched us from the lid of a dumpster.

When I was done he didn't say anything for a long time.

Then he said, "You should have told me a year ago."

"I couldn't."

"You could. You decided not to. There's a difference."

I didn't have an answer.

He said, "Don't come back here. Don't write. Don't visit. If they ever assign you to me, do it cleanly."

He left.

I stood in the alley for a long time. The cat eventually got tired of watching me and went into the dumpster.

I noticed, walking back, that the truth had not made me feel cleaner. The truth had made me feel like a person who had told the truth for the wrong reasons — because keeping the secret was finally heavier than I wanted to carry.

That was a different kind of weakness. I made a note of it.

t2.4.text:
The man's name was Andrei Kos. He was forty-three. He was a high school chemistry teacher.

He had been flagged by the Council's predictive system for *associative dissent* — meaning he had eaten dinner three times in the last six months with a man who had been flagged for *direct dissent*. The direct dissenter, as far as I could tell from the case file on my tablet, had once written an opinion piece about Council water policy. The opinion piece had never even cleared the moderation queue.

We took Kos at his apartment, at nine in the evening, in front of his children. He had two of them. A daughter, ten. A son, six. They were in their pajamas. The son had Eques stickers on his pajamas. He pointed at our uniforms and said, *"They're like in my book,"* and the daughter looked at her father's face and started to cry before anything had happened, because she was ten and she had figured out, faster than her brother, what kind of book it was actually like.

Kos was quiet at first. Then, when the senior Skiritai started reading the warrant off his tablet, he was less quiet. He begged. He did the begging cleanly — no dignity stuff, no *I demand a lawyer*, no *you're making a mistake*. Just *please. Please. The kids.*

I had to put a hand on his shoulder. Then both hands. Then my arms around him, when he tried to drop to the floor. The body camera clipped to my collar recorded all of it. The audio analysis would parse it for the threat-assessment review in the morning.

He weighed a lot less than I'd expected.

I kept my face arranged in the shape we'd been told to keep our faces in. Calm. Professional. The shape was easy. I had been practicing.

In the transport van, on the way back, the senior Skiritai looked at me and said, "First one?"

I said, "Yes, sir."

He said, "You'll get used to it."

I noticed he had used the exact phrase the Eques in the car had not used, two years before, when I was the one in the back seat.

I noticed I was now on the other side of that sentence.

t2.5.text:
A week later. A biometric checkpoint at the south end of the Capitol wall. A young man, my age, ran.

He shouldn't have run. The checkpoint was a routine secondary screening — facial-rec had returned an inconclusive match for someone the system was looking for, which was an everyday occurrence, and the worst that happens to a probable is a forty-minute interview and then they reissue your day-pass. He'd run anyway. People do, sometimes. The body does what the body does.

The senior Skiritai — the same one who'd told me I'd get used to it — yelled my name through the earpiece comms.

"Mika! Drop him!"

The runner was forty meters out and accelerating. The drone overhead was already adjusting its trajectory to follow.

I had a service rifle. It was not a difficult shot.

conditionalText.append: *For one second, looking at the back of him as he ran — same height, same lankiness, same way the elbows pumped — I thought he was Quinn. He wasn't. Quinn was three Quadrants away in a warehouse with a shaved head. But the second was there, and it was a long second, and it stained everything that came after it.*

t2.5_kill.text:
I fired once.

He went down at thirty-eight meters. He was dead before he hit the ground, which I knew because I'd taken the shot at the base of the skull and that is the kind of shot that does not leave room for interpretation.

The CO put a hand on my shoulder.

"Clean," he said. "Good."

We walked over. The drone was already photographing the body for the incident report. The runner had been carrying nothing. Nothing in his pockets, nothing in his hands, nothing in the small pack on his back except a sandwich and a paperback book whose title I didn't recognize. The Council's predictive system had returned an inconclusive match because he had searched the term *unauthorized assembly* on a public library terminal three weeks before. He had run because he had panicked. He was nineteen years old.

Cleanup arrived in an unmarked van. We filed the report from the field — the CO did the typing, I did the body-cam upload. The CO walked me back to the station and bought me dinner from the staff cafeteria, which was the protocol — first kills got dinner, the way first periods got chocolate, in some other version of childhood that wasn't mine.

I ate the dinner. I slept that night. I was surprised at how well I slept.

That was the worst part.

t2.5_wound.text:
I aimed low. I hit him in the left thigh.

He went down screaming. He kept trying to crawl. He didn't get far.

The CO was furious. He shouted at me — *clean shot, Mika, clean shot, that was a clean shot* — and then he walked over and finished the job himself, two rounds, very fast, and the runner stopped crawling. The drone overhead got both kills on video.

The CO came back. He didn't look at me.

He said, "If you're going to be squeamish, be squeamish at home. Not on shift. Not on the body cam."

He didn't write me up. He didn't escalate. He told me, at the end of the shift, that he understood — *first one's hard* — and he hoped I'd be more decisive next time.

I walked back to the station alone. I told myself, on the walk, that I had not killed the boy. The CO had killed the boy. I had only wounded him. There was a moral difference. There had to be.

The moral difference would last me about four months.

t2.5_refuse.endingTitle: Ending #7: Executed by the Council (field variant)
t2.5_refuse.text:
I lowered the rifle.

The CO was twelve feet to my right. He saw it. Everyone saw it. The drone overhead saw it. The body cam clipped to my collar saw it.

He didn't yell again. He drew his sidearm, unhurried, the way you draw a sidearm when you have absolutely decided what comes next. He shot me.

Once, in the stomach. I went down.

He stood over me. He shot me twice more, both in the chest, and he said the thing he had to say for the incident report — *insubordination under fire, terminated in field* — and the runner got away in the meantime, which was the part that, lying on the asphalt, I was most pleased about.

I had time, before everything went, to be pleased about it.

It was not a long time.

*The runner — never identified, the drone footage of his face inconclusive — escaped into the third Quadrant. He kept running. He did not stop running for a long time. Bran would be told you died honorably. Quinn was still alive in the warehouse. He would be alive for nine more months. After that, the file closed itself.*

t2.5_shoot_co.endingTitle: Ending #7: Executed by the Council (defiance variant)
t2.5_shoot_co.text:
I turned and shot the CO.

I don't fully remember the decision. I remember the body deciding before the mind did. I remember the rifle coming up to a different target than the one it had been aimed at. I remember the CO's expression, which was not surprise — it was something more like recognition. Like he had always thought it would be one of his recruits eventually.

He went down. The runner kept running, and made it past the drone's last clean capture.

I had four minutes before the response unit arrived. I used them to disable my body camera and pull the GPS chip out of my badge, because I knew the system better now than I had a year ago.

I made it three blocks before they cornered me.

I don't remember most of what happened after that. I remember a doorway. I remember someone's hand on the back of my neck. I remember thinking, very clearly, *Quinn would have laughed at this.*

I was wrong about most of my last thoughts. I was right about that one.

*The story of a Skiritai who shot his own CO and let a probable escape was scrubbed from the official record but kept circulating on the encrypted forums in the third Quadrant for a year before it was forgotten. You became, briefly, a story. You never knew.*

t2.6.text:
I was twelve. Bran was nine.

We were walking home from school, and Bran was eating one of those pickled things from the corner stand, the ones Mom hated because they made his uniform smell. I was making fun of him about it. He was laughing in the way nine-year-olds laugh when they're trying to chew and laugh at the same time, badly.

The afternoon light was gold. The street was warm. There was a woman at the second-floor window of the green building hanging laundry, and the laundry caught the light, and a dog crossed in front of us and Bran tried to pet it and it went past, and we got to the corner of our street and Bran said something about a cartoon he'd seen, and I said something dismissive about the cartoon, and he laughed at me for being dismissive about a cartoon I had not even watched, and we went inside, and that was the day.

That was the whole day.

It came back to me in the transport van after the shooting. The whole afternoon. In one piece. Like a photograph someone slid under a door.

I sat in the van and I thought about the woman with the laundry, and I thought about the dog, and I thought about Bran trying to laugh and chew at the same time, and I did not cry, because I had stopped crying eight months ago, but I had a feeling in my chest like crying, which was perhaps a thing I would have to learn to live with.

t2.7.text:
A year. Then most of another year.

I will not bore us both with the year. There were more checkpoints. There were more reports. There were people I detained whose names I have not chosen to remember, although the system remembers them, in a database I now had partial access to. There were three more people I shot, two of whom died, and one of whom didn't, and the one who didn't was a woman in her fifties who I think about more often than the two who did.

Sophie and I trained. Sophie and I ate. Sophie and I became close in the slow particular way of people who have agreed not to talk about the work. We did not talk about the work. We did not have to. The work was something we both did and both were good at and neither of us, by the end of that year, was the same kind of person we'd been at the start.

She told me once, slightly drunk on Capitol-issue wine, that her father had said I was *promising*. She'd said it like she was warning me.

I'd thanked her for the warning.

The promotion came in the fall. Achaean black-and-gold. The tailor had measured me a week before and the uniform fit. The service pistol I now knew how to use. The biometric clearance bumped two levels — Achaeans could move through doors that Skiritai couldn't see. There was a small ceremony. There was applause that didn't touch the walls.

The Achaean who would be my squad leader stepped out of the row at the back to shake my hand.

He had pale eyes and a burn scar that started under his jaw and went down into his collar.

I had seen him before. Twice. Once in the gym, when I was fifteen. Once at the back of my Skiritai induction, watching me, nodding once.

He shook my hand. He held it a beat longer than necessary.

He said, "Welcome. I'm Jason."

I had been waiting two years to hear him say his name.

I had not known I was waiting until I heard it.

t3.1.text:
Jason's office was on the fourth floor of the Achaean wing, in a corner with two windows. The Capitol lights came in at a low angle in the evenings and made the whole room the color of old paper — which was strange, given how little paper was in the room. He had two armchairs. He had a kettle on a hot plate. He had a workstation with three monitors that he kept turned off when he was meeting with people. He had a bookshelf — actual physical books, which was rare anywhere in the Capitol and especially rare in an Eques's office — that I would later realize was curated, not for show, but with the precision of a man who had been thinking for a long time about what was on it. On the lowest shelf, in a worn leather binding, was a paperback copy of *Meditations*. He took it down on my first night.

"Read this," he said. "The job is hard. This helps."

He poured me tea. Black tea, the kind that tasted like wood. He didn't ask if I wanted it.

"How's your family?" he said.

I had been expecting a debrief. I had been expecting strategy. I had been expecting the kind of opening conversation a senior officer has with his new subordinate. I had not been expecting *how's your family,* delivered like he'd known me twenty years.

I was at the desk now. The man behind the desk was the man who had nodded at me from the back of two ceremonies. He had been watching me, in person and on the system, for two years.

I had to choose how to answer.

choices.label: Tell him about Bran. Be honest.

t3.1_open.text:
I told him about Bran.

I told him the way I'd told Sophie — the fractions on the screen, the rice, the bright thing he was learning not to do. I didn't know why I was telling him. I had decided, on the way up the stairs, to be careful. Then I'd sat down and my mouth had done something else.

He listened the way he poured tea. Steady. Unhurried. As if listening were a discipline like marksmanship and he had been practicing it longer than I had been alive.

When I was done he said, "He sounds like a good kid."

"He is."

"Don't bring him here. Not for visits. Not for anything. Once he's badged into an Eques facility he's in the database forever. The kindest thing you can do for a sibling, in this work, is to keep them in a different version of your life."

He said it without any weight. The way you'd tell someone to wear a coat in the rain.

I drank the tea. I knew, drinking it, that I had just given a man I did not know enough about my brother for him to find Bran in the public records system inside of forty seconds.

I knew that. I drank the tea anyway.

That was a new kind of knowing.

t3.1_deflect.text:
"They're well, sir."

He looked at me over the rim of his cup. The look had the particular kindness of a man who had asked a real question and been answered with a wall, and who had decided not to mind.

"Glad to hear it," he said.

He didn't ask again. He moved on. He talked, instead, about the work — the schedule, the squad, the kind of operations Achaeans ran. He was warm and clear and I was, against my better judgment, charmed.

I noticed, walking back to my quarters, that I would have liked him to ask again.

I noticed that I had wanted to be asked again.

It was a small thing. It worried me anyway.

t3.1_lie.text:
I gave him a tragic backstory.

I had several. I rotated them with senior officers the way you rotate stories at parties. This one was the *parents-died-young* version, which I'd given to Sophie a year before — clean, simple, ends conversations.

Jason listened. He set his cup down. He waited a full beat after I was done.

Then he said, "Mika. I knew your father. He was a baker. He's still alive. He lives on the third floor of the green building on Levant Street. His unit number is 308. He buys bread flour from the wholesaler on Wednesdays. I was the one who pulled your file when the recruiting Eques flagged you."

He took a sip of tea.

"You don't have to tell me about your family," he said. "But please don't lie to me about it. The records are right there. The lying is what I find difficult."

I sat there.

I had never, in my life, been more clearly outclassed by a man being kind to me.

I would think, later, that this was the moment Jason had decided to make me into something. Not by punishing the lie. By correcting it gently and then drinking his tea. He was teaching me, in real time, how *he* did this work. The work being: knowing more than the other person, and being kind about it, and watching the other person change shape under the weight of being known.

I would learn it. Of course I would learn it. He was a good teacher.

t3.2.text:
I had my own office now. It was small. By the end of the first week, three things were waiting for my attention. Three things to do, on top of the actual work.

The first was a calendar invite. No subject line. Jason had said, on my way out of his office, that I was welcome any evening. *Evenings* was his word, said the way other people say *whenever*. He was offering. He was inviting. He was teaching.

The second was a sealed assignment file on my secure terminal, marked with a code I had been briefed to recognize. My first independent operation. My first kill.

The third was a hand-written note slipped under my door overnight. Physical paper, no digital trail. The handwriting was Kaden's. I had not seen Kaden in eight months.

Three things. A month before the next promotion review. I had to choose how many I'd open.

t3.3a.text:
We walked, mostly. He wasn't a desk talker. He liked the high balcony on the east wing where you could see the city spread out below — the Quadrants in the distance, the wall, the surveillance towers, the lights of the third Quadrant where Quinn was still in a warehouse with a shaved head.

He talked about everything except the work, until he was ready to talk about the work.

When he was ready, it was on the third walk. We were standing at the rail. He said, "What do you think the Council is for, Mika?"

I had practiced answers. I had been practicing answers for two years.

He held up a hand before I started. "I'm not asking what you think the right answer is. I'm asking what you actually think."

I told him I thought it was for control.

"Control of what?"

"Of people."

"That's a lazy answer. Control of *what about* people?"

I didn't have a fast answer.

He waited. Then: "The Council exists to keep eight million people from killing each other. The thing it controls is the *appetite for chaos.* If you ever come to think the Council is the bad guy, ask yourself who keeps the bakery open. Ask yourself who makes sure the trains run. Ask yourself why your brother is alive."

"I used to think the way you think. I'm not insulting you. I'm telling you it's a phase. Most people grow out of it by twenty. The smart ones grow out of it earlier."

The drone above us made its slow circuit and continued past.

t3.3a_argue.text:
I said, "My brother is alive because my mother kept him alive. Not the Council. The Council just hasn't killed him yet."

Jason looked at me. He didn't get angry. He got *quieter*.

"That's a real answer," he said. "You're going to be a problem."

"Sir?"

"I mean that as a compliment. Mostly."

He left me at the rail. He went back inside.

I noticed I was glad I had argued. I noticed that he had let me argue, and that he had walked away looking pleased. I noticed, in other words, that I had won the argument and that I had also, somehow, lost.

t3.3a_agree.text:
I said, "I think you're right."

I said it because, standing at the rail with him, I thought — for a few full seconds — that he *was* right. The bakery. The trains. The brother. He had laid it out and the lay-out was clean.

I would not realize, until much later, that this had been the goal.

Jason smiled. A small, real smile. "Good. The job is much easier when you let yourself be right."

The next morning I signed three forms before breakfast. I signed them without slowing down. I did not feel anything when I signed them.

I told myself this was efficiency.

t3.3a_perform.text:
I said, "I think you're right."

I didn't think he was right. But the conversation was a test, and I had decided I would be the kind of subordinate who passed gentle tests.

I said it well. I said it with the right hesitation. I said it like a smart kid trying to admit, against his pride, that an older man had a point.

He clapped me on the shoulder.

He was not stupid. There was a possibility — small but real — that he had heard exactly what I'd done and had clapped me on the shoulder anyway. Because performing the thing well was, in his world, sometimes good enough. The performance was the thing he was teaching.

I would think about that, later. More than I wanted to.

t3.3b.text:
The target was a forty-year-old man named Ennis Ardin. He taught literature at a high school in the second Quadrant. He had hosted three private discussion groups in the last year about Council policy. The Council's predictive system had flagged the gatherings as *pre-emergent dissident formation.* The flag had been upgraded to *active.* The active flag had landed on my desk.

He had a wife and a five-year-old daughter. They were sleeping in the next room.

I stood outside his apartment door at three in the morning with a lockpick kit, a service pistol with a suppressor, and a Council key card. The corridor camera had been disabled remotely from my terminal. The corridor was empty.

I had four options.

choices.label: Clean kill — do it right, get out.
Messy kill — make it look like a robbery.
Fake the death — let him escape, file the report.

t3.3b_clean.text:
I went in.

He was sleeping on his side. I checked the room. Nothing on the nightstand worth taking. I took two steps to the bed. I waited until he turned, and the turn put him face-up.

The shot was clean. The suppressor did its work.

I was out of the building in eleven minutes. I re-enabled the corridor camera as I left.

The wife found him at six.

I filed the report. *Target neutralized, no collateral.* Jason signed it without looking up. "Good work."

I slept four hours. In the morning I noticed I was not different. I had killed a man — the deliberate one, the planned one — and I had thought it would change me visibly.

The face in the mirror was the same.

That was the thing that worried me. Not the kill. The sameness.

t3.3b_messy.text:
I made it look like a break-in.

A clean kill was a *Council* kill, and everyone in the second Quadrant would know. A robbery gone wrong was a story people could tell themselves. The Council preferred the clean version. Sometimes I preferred the other.

I tossed the apartment. Broke a window. Took a watch and some cash. Left a knife in the doorframe. Shot him from across the room, twice.

The wife screamed long enough that the neighbors heard her before I was out.

I filed the report. *Target encountered intruder during assigned window. Operation completed under cover of incident.*

Jason looked at me a long time after reading it. "Why this way?"

I gave him the logic.

He said, "Be careful with stories, Mika. Stories are how you start lying to yourself."

He signed the report. I didn't sleep that night.

t3.3b_fake.text:
I went in. I woke him up.

I covered his mouth before he could shout. I held the suppressor at his temple. I leaned down, very close, and told him exactly what was going to happen.

I would fire into the pillow next to his head. He would go limp. He would wait two full minutes. Then he would get up, take his daughter and his wife, leave through the back fire escape — the one with no camera coverage, I had checked — and never come back. He would not return. He would not write. He would not contact anyone he had hosted in his living room.

He nodded, as well as he could with my hand on his mouth.

I fired into the pillow. Left through the front, slowly.

I filed the report. *Target neutralized. No collateral.*

The wife and the daughter and Ennis Ardin were on a transport out of the Capitol by morning, riding under names I had generated using a Council ID terminal I shouldn't have had access to.

Jason signed the report. He didn't look at it twice.

I had bought myself a thing. A man's life, and his daughter's, and his wife's. I had also bought myself a *secret* — the kind of secret that, in this work, would eventually get me killed if I was not careful.

I decided I would be careful.

t3.3b_abort.text:
I didn't go in.

I stood in the corridor. The lockpick was in my hand. The pistol was at my hip. I stood there for ninety seconds, until a movement light at the end of the hallway clicked on and clicked off, and then I left.

I filed the report as *target's premises compromised — operational integrity insufficient — recommend reassignment.* Standard language. Plausible.

Jason read it across the desk. He didn't say anything for a long time.

Then: "Mika. The next assignment you receive, you will complete. If you don't, I will not protect you. I am protecting you now. That's why we're having this conversation. There won't be another."

He signed the report. He looked at me one more time before I left.

"I'm not angry. I'm disappointed. And disappointed is more dangerous, in this building, than angry. Find a way."

I walked back to my office. I sat at the desk a long time.

I noticed I was not relieved. I had refused — and I felt, on a physical level, *worse* than I had felt on the runner-with-the-sandwich morning.

That was new information. I filed it away.

t3.3c.text:
The note said an address and a time. The address was a Capitol bar I had never been to — quietly mapped on the internal Eques mapping tool as a known camera-blind-spot establishment. The time was eleven at night.

Kaden looked worse than I had expected.

He had been promoted to Achaean too. Same year. We had not crossed paths, because he had been assigned to a different branch — internal ops, the kind that handled Eques who had become problems for other Eques.

He was thinner. Hollow under the eyes. The boy from the mess hall had been *worn.* This man was *cracked.*

But the mind was sharp. The mind was, if anything, sharper.

He bought me a drink. He didn't drink one himself.

"I have a plan, Just Mika. I want to tell it to you. It's going to take five years. It's going to require both of us to stay alive, which is the unpleasant part. And it's going to put us, by the time we're twenty-two, in a position to kill a Hetairoi and replace them."

The smile didn't reach the rest of his face.

"Tell me you're listening."

choices.label: Pretend to agree. Plan to use him.

t3.3c_agree.text:
I said, "I'm listening."

He told me the plan.

It was good. Startlingly good. It involved patience and patience and patience and then a single afternoon. Positioning ourselves in two specific Achaean tracks. Then two Agema seats. Then eligibility for a Hetairoi vacancy that would open between our twenty-second and twenty-third birthdays.

The Hetairoi who would die was a man named Tellis. Kaden had reasons. The reasons were not just pragmatic. They were also *personal* in a way I did not press him on, and would, later, regret not pressing him on.

I said yes.

I said yes for several reasons. One was that I wanted the seat. One was that I wanted Kaden to have something to live for. One was that I had not, until I sat across from him, realized how much I had missed having a friend who *understood* the work.

We shook on it. Walking back, I noticed I had just agreed to murder a Hetairoi.

I noticed I was looking forward to it.

t3.3c_resist.text:
I let him tell the plan. I listened to all of it.

When he was done I said, "Kaden. You haven't slept in a month."

"I sleep."

"You haven't slept *well* in a month."

The cracked eyes did a tired thing.

"Don't do this," he said. "Don't be the friend who tries to save me."

"I'm not trying to save you. I'm trying to tell you the plan is a plan a person makes when they're not sleeping."

We sat in the bar another twenty minutes. I bought him food. He ate some of it. We talked about other things — old training jokes, a mutual instructor we had both disliked, the weather. The weather was a bad sign in itself.

I walked him home. I made him take a glass of water. I sat with him until he fell asleep on the couch.

I noticed *clean* was a feeling I had not had in some time and that I was suspicious of it.

t3.3c_use.text:
I said, "Tell me everything."

He told me. The plan. The timeline. The Hetairoi. The names of the men he had been quietly building rapport with for two years. He told me with the particular clarity of a man who had been holding the plan in his head alone for too long.

I listened. I asked good questions. The questions a partner would ask.

I was not a partner.

I was already, while he was talking, working out how to turn the plan to my own ends. He had named four officers in our cohort whose loyalties he believed he had secured. In the next month, I would quietly check on each of them. If any of them turned out to be more useful to me alone than to us together, I would adjust.

I let him buy me dinner. I told him I was glad he'd brought it to me first.

That part wasn't a lie.

I had a friend who had brought me a gift, and I was already, in my head, putting away the parts of the gift I would keep for myself.

t3.4_leverage.text:
A sealed file appeared in my secure terminal. The seal was Hetairoi-grade — they came down through Agema first, normally. This one came directly.

Inside was a digital photograph and a single page.

The photograph was Quinn. Older. Gaunt. They had shaved his head a long time ago and the hair had been growing back in patches.

The page read:

*Achaean Mika.*

*Subject Q. Reilan, file open and pending. Clearance to close has been delegated to your office, contingent on two conditions: 1) the file is closed in the Council's preferred direction, and 2) the file is closed by your hand.*

*We are aware of the message you sent in your sixteenth year. We have made no record of it. We expect, in return, your continued cooperation.*

It was signed *L. Vespera.*

I read it three times. The third time, I noticed my hands were not shaking.

A year ago, my hands would have been shaking.

I had been *seen* — fully seen, by Vespera, who had not yet met me — and the seeing had been, in its way, a kind of recruitment.

t3.4_alive.text:
A sealed file appeared in my secure terminal.

Inside was a digital photograph and a single page.

Quinn. Older. Gaunt. Shaved head growing back in patches. His eyes were the same.

The page read:

*File: Q. Reilan. Status: open, holding, third-Quadrant facility 14-B. No action required at this time. Distributed to your office for situational awareness.*

He was alive. He was still in the same facility. The file had moved to my level — not because I had asked, but because the system, having processed me up the ranks, was now sharing things with me that had previously been kept from me.

I closed the file. I sat at my desk for a long time.

I had, until this moment, told myself that I would help Quinn when I had the rank to help him. I now had something close to the rank. I did not, in fact, have anything resembling a *plan* to help him. I had a plan to *eventually* have a plan.

*Eventually* had been a comfortable word for two years.

t3.4_dead.text:
A sealed file appeared in my secure terminal.

*File: Q. Reilan. Status: CLOSED.*

*Resolution date: 06-04 of the prior year.*

He had been dead for nine months.

Closed. *Closed* was the word, and *closed* was the word I had learned, three years before, had two meanings. He had not been released. He had been killed quietly during one of the periodic file reviews, and no one had told me, because I was not — at that level of clearance — a person who needed to be told.

I sat at the desk.

I had told myself, for two years, that I would find him when I had the rank. The rank had now arrived. The boy was nine months gone.

I noticed I was not crying. I noticed I had been ready to cry — the throat had done the small tightening — and that the tightening had not finished. The body had stopped halfway and waited to see what I would do.

I did not do anything.

The body relaxed. It had learned what to expect from me.

t3.4_dead_shock.text:
A sealed file appeared in my secure terminal. It was not unusual. Achaeans got these all day.

*File: Q. Reilan. Status: CLOSED.*

*Resolution date: 06-04 of the prior year.*

I read the name three times before I understood whose file it was.

I had not, in the year and a half since the morale report, allowed myself to think Quinn's full name. The full name — *Quinn Reilan* — felt, on the screen, like a stranger's.

He had been dead for nine months and I had not known. I had not searched. I had not investigated. I had told myself, for two years, that I would find him when I had the rank. The rank had now arrived. The boy was nine months gone, and I had not even bothered to look in time to know.

I sat at the desk.

This time the body did cry. Briefly. Two minutes. I let it. Then I cleaned my face. Then I went to dinner.

At dinner I ate normally. I made conversation with two other Achaeans. I laughed at a joke.

The cry had been a release valve and the release valve had worked, and I would not cry again.

t3.5.text:
I was fifteen.

I was in the hallway, walking home from school, taking the wrong corridor on purpose, because I had told Quinn I'd meet him at the upper east stairs and the upper east stairs were a slightly longer route and I had the time.

The door was half-open. I looked through it because I am the kind of person who looks through doors. That has, perhaps, been the whole problem.

I saw the screen. I saw Quinn's face. I saw the file.

The Eques was at the desk. He turned. His eyes met mine.

I had remembered this moment many times. I had remembered it from inside my own body.

This time, sitting at my desk in my Achaean office, I was not in my own body. I was floating above the hallway. Looking down on the boy in the doorway.

He looked very small.

The Eques at the desk turned his head. I saw the Eques's face, this time, and the face was not the face I remembered. The face I remembered had been older, taller, harder. The face I was looking at now was younger. Approximately seventeen.

I knew the face.

It was mine.

t3.6.text:
The intelligence came from Kaden, eventually.

The shape of it was this:

The Council had a triage process for flagged citizen files. The most serious went to Vespera and her inner circle. The trivial ones — literature teachers, pamphleteers, boys who had once searched the wrong term — went to a single Achaean who had been doing the work, by personal preference, for nine years.

The Achaean processed the trivial cases quietly. He kept them small, and quiet, and *clean.* Without him, the whole architecture of *minor* enforcement — the architecture that kept the Council looking less brutal than it was — would collapse upward, until the Council's daily work became so visibly cruel that even the people who tolerated it now would have to stop.

The Achaean was Jason.

If Jason died, the system did not have a replacement of his caliber. In three to five years, the architecture would crack. The cracks would force change.

To fix the city, I had to kill the kindest man I had ever met.

I went to his office that night. He was reading. The kettle was on the hot plate.

He looked up when I came in. He saw the suppressor on my pistol. He did not look surprised.

He set the book down. "Sit, Mika. Let's at least have the tea."

conditionalText.append: *I had spent months arguing with him on the high balcony. I knew, in a way I had not known when I'd walked up the stairs, that there was nothing left to say.*

choices.label: Kill him cleanly. Make it quick.
Try one more time to convert him.
Let Sophie do it. You tipped her off.

t3.7a.text:
I sat. We had the tea.

He did not try to talk me out of it. He drank his tea. He asked me, twice, about Bran. He asked me about Sophie. He asked, in the small careful voice he used for hard questions, whether I had been sleeping.

I had been sleeping. I told him so.

"Good. The job doesn't go well otherwise."

conditionalText.append:
He said, "I knew it would be you, eventually. I'm glad it was you. The other candidates would have made it ugly."

I shot him once. Behind the ear. He went forward onto the desk, hand still loosely on his cup, and the cup did not even tip.

The quiet was the new shape of me.

He said, "I'd like to ask one favor. Tell my sister it was quick. Don't tell her anything else."

I told him I would.

I shot him once. Behind the ear. He went forward onto the desk, hand still loosely on his cup, and the cup did not even tip.

The quiet was the new shape of me.

t3.7b.text:
I didn't sit.

I shot him before he was done with the sentence about the tea. The first shot was high and to the left. It hit the bookshelf. He moved — fast, faster than I had expected — and went for the alarm under the desk. The second shot caught him in the shoulder. He still reached. The third shot took him in the side of the neck.

He bled out in about ninety seconds. I watched.

The room was a mess. The report I would have to file would not be clean.

I left the *Meditations* on the floor. I made it back to my quarters. I sat at my desk with my hands on the surface, and I noticed that they were shaking — not from horror, but from something more like *adrenaline.*

The shaking did not come with shame.

I noticed the absence of the shame and I was not sure whether it was because I had been right about the necessity, or because something in me had been quietly closing for two years and had now finished closing.

t3.7c.text:
I sat. We had the tea.

I told him what I had figured out. The triage. The architecture. The way his quiet work held up the whole charade. I asked him if he had ever considered that he was, in fact, the most *dangerous* man in the building.

He listened the way he always listened. He did not interrupt.

When I was done he said, "Yes. I've considered it."

I waited.

"If I leave this office tonight, the city is worse by morning. The Council will not collapse. The Council will *escalate.* They will replace me clumsily. The clumsy replacement will land on people Vespera doesn't currently bother with. Your brother. Your friend Niko. I have been keeping the Council *small* for nine years. I am the only thing keeping the brutality from being twenty times what it is now."

He set down his cup. He looked at me with sorrow.

"You're going to kill me anyway. Aren't you."

t3.7d.text:
I lowered the gun.

I tried to. The hand started the motion. The arm was almost halfway down, and I was already beginning to feel the relief of *not having done it,* when Jason's other hand came up from under the desk with the small black sidearm he had been carrying for nine years.

He was not surprised. He was sad.

conditionalText.append:
He said, "We both knew this was where it was going. I'm proud of you, for the part where you tried."

He held my hand at the end.

He said, "I'm going to tell them you came at me. I owe you that much."

He did not look at me as he said it. He was already drafting the report in his head.

t3.7e.text:
I did not go to Jason's office that night.

I had told Sophie, three weeks before, the shape of what I had figured out. I had told her over a long dinner. I had told her in the way you tell a friend who you know will, when they hear the conclusion, want to be the one to do the thing.

Sophie was, in many ways, a simpler person than I was. The simplicity was not a flaw. It was an advantage I did not yet fully understand.

She went that night. I knew because I saw the small movement of a single light at four in the morning, in the corner where Jason's office was, and I saw the light go out twenty minutes later.

I slept.

In the morning the report on Jason's death was filed by Sophie. *Insurgent activity, lone target, neutralized.* The Council praised her work. She was promoted to Agema in front of me, three weeks later. I was kept Achaean.

I had given Jason to Sophie because giving him to Sophie was easier than killing him myself.

I kept this in a small private file in the back of my head, marked *SOFTNESS,* and I did not look at it again for almost two years.

When I did look at it again, the cost would be considerably higher than the seat.

t3.8_promotion.text:
I was promoted to Agema in the autumn.

The grey coat was heavy. Real wool. The medals on the chest came in two rows, three on each row, and the third row would come in a year if I was promoted again, and the row after that if I made Hetairoi.

The ceremony was small. Four of us promoted. The Hetairoi who pinned my insignia spoke to me by name, called me by *first name*, which she should not have been able to do without consulting a document. She did not consult a document.

Vespera did not pin the insignia. She was not running the ceremony. But she was in the room.

She was in dove-grey, the way she always was. She watched me. She had not yet spoken to me. She had been, by my count, watching me for three years.

She caught my eye, once. She gave me a small smile. The smile was not warm. It was the smile of a woman who had decided I was the next one.

I understood the smile.

I would think later that the smile was the moment I had known what I was going to do, even if I had not yet known I knew.

t3.8_demoted.text:
The promotion ceremony was small. Four of us. Sophie was one of them.

I was not.

I stood in the audience. I clapped at the right moments. I had been told, very privately, that I was *deferred* — not denied, deferred.

Sophie smiled at me from the dais. She did not know — she could not know — that I had given this to her. She thought she had earned it.

She had earned it. The version of *earning* that this place rewarded, she had earned.

I noticed I was not jealous. I noticed I was something else. The something else had the quiet of *patience*, but it also had the temperature of *anger*, and the two of them braided together to make a thing I had not had before.

t4.1_signing.text:
The Agema desk was polished black wood. A green leather inlay. A small silver pen-holder. Three leather-bound dossiers, fresh from intake, on a tray to my right.

I opened the first.

The man was named Edrik Vant. I had never heard of him. A logistics coordinator for a Quadrant transit company. Flagged for *facilitation of association* — meaning his transit corridor had been used by a group the Council was watching. There was no evidence he had known.

There was a checkbox at the bottom of the form. The checkbox said *EXECUTE.*

I checked the box. I signed. I moved to the next.

The second was a woman in her thirties. She had hosted a discussion group like the one Ennis Ardin had hosted. I checked the box. I signed.

The third was Marya Fennik.

Marya Fennik had been my math tutor when I was thirteen. She had sat at my mother's kitchen table for forty minutes once a week for two years and had taught me trigonometry with a patience that I had, at the time, taken for granted. She had laughed at my jokes when they were not funny. She had brought my mother bread.

She had been flagged for *known association* — she had attended a single meeting of a Quadrant labor group.

I read the file twice.

choices.label: Pause. Pull her full file. Read it.
Don't pause. Move to the next.

t4.1_demoted_intro.text:
I was Achaean another year. The year was a long quiet thing.

I did my work. I outmaneuvered three other Achaean candidates the slow way — the way Vespera had outmaneuvered her own contemporaries, four decades before. The slow way is not glamorous. It is not satisfying. It is a series of small choices that add up to a position.

A year and a half later, the seat opened. I took it.

I was promoted to Agema at nineteen and a half, instead of nineteen.

The half was, in the Council's records, immaterial. It was not immaterial to me. It was the cost of the moral argument I had made in Jason's office. I would be paying it, in small ways, for years.

The Agema desk was polished black wood. The third file on it was Marya Fennik — my math tutor when I was thirteen. She had been flagged for *known association.*

choices.label: Pause. Pull her full file. Read it.
Don't pause. Move to the next.

t4.1_pause.text:
I pulled the file.

Her full file was forty pages. I read all of it. She had attended the meeting because the woman she was seeing — a janitor in a third-Quadrant administrative building — had asked her to come. She had not spoken at the meeting. She had not returned.

I sat at the desk for a long time.

I had two options now, neither of which was new. I could sign. I could not sign.

I signed.

The pause did not change the outcome. The pause was the difference between *signing* and *signing while having read the file,* and the difference between those two things was a very small comfort I gave to myself, in a private chamber of my head where I kept the small comforts.

I noticed the chamber was getting full.

t4.1_move.text:
I checked the box.

I did it without rereading the name. The pen had been moving down the page. The pen continued to move down the page. The signature was the same signature I had put on every other order that morning, a clean *M Halren* with a small underscore, and the underscore did its work, and Marya Fennik was now, on this day at this desk, a signed order.

I went on to the next file.

I noticed, three or four files later, that I had not paused. I noticed it the way you notice a thing about a stranger.

Her name had not stopped me.

That was useful information about myself.

I did not let myself think anything else about it for the rest of the day.

t4.2.text:
The Agema office was on the seventh floor. The window looked east, across the wall, at the Quadrants where I had grown up. On a clear day you could see, just barely, the tower of the green building on Levant Street where my mother still lived.

She had moved a year ago. She had not told me where.

There were three things on my list this month.

A hand-written invitation had arrived from Vespera's office. Tea. Her chambers. *Whenever convenient.* Tea with Vespera was not a thing one declined.

Kaden was unraveling. He had been unraveling for a year, and I had been pretending the unraveling was not accelerating. The acceleration was no longer deniable.

And it had been three years since I had been to my mother's apartment. Bran was now fifteen. Niko was organizing labor protests in the second Quadrant in a way that suggested he had decided to live a much shorter life than was strictly necessary.

Three things. A few weeks. I had to choose.

choices.label: Cultivate Lady Vespera. Accept her invitation.
Final visit to the old neighborhood.

t4.3a.text:
Her chambers were on the top floor of the Hetairoi tower. Two rooms. The first was a small white parlor with two armchairs. There was no decoration except a single arrangement of dove-grey winter flowers on a low table. The flowers were real.

She rose when I came in. She poured the tea herself.

She was tall. Slender. White-and-cream silk. Her silver-white hair was up in a soft architectural style I had not seen before, and would not see again — she did not, I would learn, ever wear her hair the same way twice. The half-smile was on her face the way it was always on her face.

"Sit, Mika."

I sat. We had the tea.

She said, "Tell me what you actually want."

She said it the way Jason had said *how's your family.* Without weight. As if it had not, in fact, been the entire purpose of the meeting.

I had been preparing for this conversation for three years.

choices.label: Tell her you want her seat.
Tell her you want to fix the city.
Tell her you want her approval.

t4.3a_ambition.text:
"I want your seat," I said.

She laughed. A small, real laugh. The first time I had ever heard her produce a sound that was not, in some way, calibrated.

"Of course you do," she said. "I would have lost interest already if you didn't. The interesting question is how *cleanly* you want it."

"Cleanly?"

"Some men want my seat by killing me. Some by replacing me. Some by being so useful that I retire and hand it to them out of fondness. All three are possible. I would like to know which you are."

I had not, going in, decided which I was.

I gave her the best answer I had: I told her I had not decided yet.

She seemed pleased. "Smart. The ones who decide too early always pick the wrong one. You and I are going to be friends, Mika. I think we have several years of being friends ahead of us."

She refilled my cup.

I noticed, leaving her chambers, that I had been more honest with her than I had been with anyone in two years. I noticed she had liked it.

t4.3a_idealism.text:
"I want to fix the city," I said.

She did not laugh.
