She set down her cup very gently. She looked at me, for a long moment, with what I would have called sympathy if I had not known her better.

"I see," she said. "How sweet."

She did not say it cruelly. She said it the way an aunt might say *how sweet* to a child's drawing of a horse. With a kind of sincere fondness for the *attempt.*

"I felt that way, when I was your age. I did. I was very serious about it. I came to the conclusion, eventually, that the city is not a thing one *fixes.* The city is a thing one *manages.* Once you understand that, the work becomes easier, and you become better at it."

She drank.

"You will come to that conclusion too, in time."

She did not seem worried about my idealism. She seemed, in fact, charmed by it. The way a person is charmed by a flower they know will be dead in a week.

t4.3a_approval.text:
"I want you to like me," I said.

The words came out before I had decided to say them. They surprised me. They surprised her, too — the half-smile shifted, briefly, into something more interested.

She put down her cup.

"That," she said, "is the most useful answer you could have given me. Most men your age would not have been able to say it."

She stood. She walked to the window. The Capitol lay below us in its evening colors.

"I will like you, Mika. I already do. I will continue to. I will, in fact, like you in a way that will be very, very difficult for you to walk back from, when the time comes that you should walk it back."

She turned. Her smile was not unkind.

"Many men have liked me. Almost none of them survived liking me. I am telling you this in the spirit of full disclosure."

I did not know what to say. I drank the tea.

She let me leave without pressing further.

She had judged correctly. That was the worst kind of manipulation. The kind that worked on you because you respected the manipulator.

t4.3a_lie.text:
"I want nothing," I said.

She held the cup at her lips. She did not drink. She lowered it again.

"Hm," she said. "That's disappointing."

She did not press me.

We finished the tea. We talked about other things — Capitol weather, a recent renovation, a piece of music she had been listening to. The conversation was *pleasant.*

She walked me to the door. At the door she paused.

"When you're ready to tell me the truth, Mika, I'll be here. I'm not in a hurry. The people who say *nothing* always come back. They always want something specific. The longer they wait to tell me what it is, the less interesting they become."

She closed the door.

I noticed that the lie had cost me something. Specifically, the version of her interest I had wanted to inherit from this conversation. I had walked in with the *grooming* version. I had walked out with the *amused-and-disappointed* version. The amused-and-disappointed version was a much harder room to climb out of.

t4.3b.text:
He had not answered my last three messages.

I let myself in with the key he had given me a year ago and had probably forgotten he had given me. The apartment was a narrow Eques two-room. He was on the floor of the living room, sitting against the couch, looking at his hands. He looked up when I came in.

He said, "Hey, Just Mika."

He said it like it had been ten minutes since we'd last talked.

He had not slept. He had not eaten. The plant on the windowsill had been dead for a long time. There were small piles of papers on the table — papers that, in our work, you did not leave on a table. File fragments. Names. Photographs.

He saw me looking.

"They visit," he said. "Sometimes. The people from the files. They sit on the couch."

He gestured at the couch.

"It's why I'm on the floor."

I sat down across from him.

I had four ways out of this conversation. None of them were good.

choices.label: Stay with him. Get him help. Risk your reputation.
Use his state. Extract what you need.
Cut him off. He's a liability now.
Offer mercy. Help him end it on his terms.

t4.3b_save.text:
I stayed.

I sat with him for two hours. He talked sometimes. He didn't talk other times. I made him eat. I made him drink water. I made him take a shower while I sat in the kitchen and pretended I was not waiting to make sure he came out again.

I called an Eques physician I had reason to trust. I did not give her his name. I gave her enough to know I was bringing in a man who was *cracked* and needed quiet handling.

I took him to her in the morning. He spent six weeks in a quiet facility. I visited every week. He came out — not whole, but functional enough to walk and eat and look at couches without flinching. He thanked me, the day he came out, in the small dry voice he had when he was sincere.

I had used a great deal of political capital to do this. The capital would cost me, later.

I noticed I had done a thing for a friend that did not in any way help me. I noticed the noticing. I noticed the noticing was, in itself, a small clean thing.

I held it carefully, walking. I would need it.

t4.3b_use.text:
I sat with him.

I made him eat. I made him drink. While he was doing those things — slowly, mechanically, with the obedience of a man who had given up the energy required to refuse — I asked him, gently, about a few of the names on the table.

He answered.

He answered *well.* He had, even in this state, the precision he had always had. He gave me details I had been working a month to find. He gave me, on one of the names, a piece of intelligence that would, six weeks later, allow me to outmaneuver an Agema rival without lifting a finger.

He did not, I think, fully realize what he was doing. He was talking the way a tired man talks when a friend asks. He was talking because he trusted me.

I left after three hours. I did not call a physician. I did not bring help.

I noticed, walking out, that he had not noticed I had not brought help.

I had just used the breakdown of my closest friend in this place to advance my career.

I noticed it and I did not, particularly, mind. There was a part of me that should have minded. The part had been quiet for a while.

t4.3b_abandon.text:
I stood.

I told him I had to go. I told him I would check on him in a few days. I gave him the small lie of *I'll be back soon* in the small voice you give that lie in.

He nodded. He didn't believe me. He did not seem hurt. He had, by that point, stopped being hurt by things.

He said, "Take care of yourself, Just Mika."

I closed the door behind me.

I did not go back. Not the next day. Not the week after. Not the month after that.

I would tell myself, later, that I had been busy.

I would also know, when I told myself this, that *busy* was a word a person used when they did not want to look at what they had done.

I would hear the front door of his apartment in my head, sometimes. The sound of it closing.

The sound was small. It carried.

t4.3b_mercy.text:
I sat with him.

I asked him, eventually, what he wanted.

He thought for a long time. The thinking did not look painful. It looked, almost, like relief.

"I want to stop being awake."

I asked him if he meant sleep.

He laughed. The laugh broke the smile, finally. The smile reached the rest of his face. The rest of his face was not, after all, dead. It was just exhausted. He looked, for one full second, like the boy in the mess hall who had told me to eat my rice.

He shook his head.

I did it that night.

It was not violent. I had been trained, by now, in many ways to end a life. I used the gentlest of them. He was holding my hand at the end. I did not let go.

I called it in as a suicide. The Council did not investigate.

I sat in my apartment. I drank water. I did not sleep.

The floor of his apartment had been the last room I had been in where I had been with someone who knew me. The full me. The full version. The hallway, the file, the boy I had been at fifteen.

There were no other rooms like that.

t4.3c.text:
I had not been to the Levant Street apartment in three years.

The bakery on the corner was still there. The dog at the second corner was a different dog. The cracked tile in front of the bakery was in the same place — the crack had grown.

My mother was at work.

Bran was in the kitchen. He was fifteen now. He had grown almost a full head taller. He was working out a calculus problem on a tablet with a chewed-end stylus. He looked up when I came in.

He didn't smile.

He looked at me like he was deciding, in real time, whether to be surprised.

"You're tall," I said.

He said, "You're in a uniform."

I had forgotten. I had thought I'd worn civilian clothes. I was wearing the uniform under a civilian jacket. The boots were Eques boots.

Niko was in the living room. He had not stood up. He had gotten older in a different way than Bran had — a way that was made of *attention,* which is what working in opposition to an empire does to a person's face.

"Hi, Mika," he said.

"Hi, Niko."

It was the first thing we had said to each other in eighteen months.

I had to choose what to say to Bran first.

choices.label: Apologize for being absent. Be present.
Don't say much. He's a kid.

t4.3c_bran_apologize.text:
I apologized.

Not for joining. For being absent. For not knowing his calculus class was algebra now. For not knowing he had grown.

He listened the way Bran listens — head slightly tilted, eyes steady. He waited until I was done.

Then he said, "Okay."

It was the same *okay* he had given me three years before at the kitchen table. The same word. The same disappointment, dressed in a more adult version of the same patience.

I noticed something in him had gotten very good at not being surprised.

That was the worst news of the visit.

t4.3c_bran_warn.text:
I told him to be careful.

Not in those words. I told him not to search anything he could not explain. I told him not to attend any gathering he had not been invited to twice. I told him that the AI listened for *patterns* now, not just words — that the way you typed, the speed, the time of day, the people you stood near in the school cafeteria, all of it.

He listened.

He said, "I know."

I looked at him. He was fifteen.

He said it again. "I know. We talk about it at school."

The kids talked about it at school. They had, apparently, been talking about it at school for some time. I had not known. I had not been to a school in four years.

t4.3c_bran_quiet.text:
I didn't say much. He's a kid.

I sat. I watched him do his calculus. I corrected one problem, the way I always had. He laughed at me a little for being a know-it-all. The laugh was not the laugh he had laughed at twelve. The laugh had been calibrated. The kid had been working on it.

I noticed I had not known he had been working on his laugh.

I noticed there was now a version of my brother I had not been around long enough to recognize, and that the version had developed in the years I had been away, and that I had no right to be sad about this.

I was sad about it anyway.

t4.3c_niko_check.text:
Niko was watching me from the living room.

I walked over. I sat down across from him.

He said, "Heard you got promoted."

"Yes."

"Big coat."

"It is."

He waited.

I had to choose what to say to him.

choices.label: Warn him — he's going to get killed if he keeps organizing.

t4.3c_niko_engage.text:
I argued with him.

I told him the Council was not what he thought. I told him the alternative was not what he thought. I told him the city as it was, was *managed,* and that managed was, at scale, the best you could hope for.

I was using Jason's words. I had not realized I knew them this well.

Niko listened. He did not interrupt. When I was done he said, "You know what's funny, Mika? You used to argue for the other side. Same heat. Same precision. Same look in the eyes."

He did not say it cruelly. He said it the way a person says, *I'm sorry for your loss.*

He stood up. He went to the kitchen. He poured himself water from the same faucet I had drunk from as a child.

We did not talk for the rest of the visit.

t4.3c_niko_warn.text:
I told him.

Not the details. Not who specifically. Just: *Niko. They have your name. The pattern of your messages. The location data from the meetings. You have, by my count, three months before they decide whether you are worth processing.*

He listened.

He said, "How do you know."

"Because I see the lists."

"Have you seen mine."

I didn't answer right away.

He waited. He had time. He had nothing but time, until they came.

I said, "Not yet."

He nodded.

He said, "If you see it before they act on it, will you tell me?"

I told him I would.

I did not know whether I would.

He knew I did not know. He thanked me anyway. He stood up. He clapped me on the shoulder. He said, "Goodbye, Mika."

He said it the way you say it to a person you have, in some private accounting, decided not to expect to see again.

t4.3c_niko_threaten.text:
I dropped the mask.

I told him the labor protests would end. I told him I would, personally, see to it that he stopped organizing, or I would personally, see to it that he was *processed.* I told him I did not have time to do this gently and that the next time I saw his name on a list I would not be in a position to remove it.

He listened. His face did not change.

He said, "You sound like one of them."

I said, "I am one of them."

He nodded. He stood up. He didn't shake my hand.

He said, "Mom won't know it was you. I'll see to that."

Then he left the apartment. The door closed gently. He was, in his way, a careful man.

I noticed I had wanted him to be angry. I had wanted the argument. I had wanted the *fight.*

What I had gotten was a man who had decided I was something he no longer had to engage with, the way you decide that about an institution.

That was a worse kind of loss than the fight would have been.

t4.4.text:
The trial was announced in early winter.

Six of us in the briefing room. All eligible Agema. Sophie was there. So was a man named Aren Volk who had hated me since basic training. So was a woman named Ilse who I did not know well. Two others I did not bother to remember.

The Hetairoi who briefed us was not Vespera. The briefing was delivered by a thin grey man named Cassius who had the air of a person who thought trials were beneath him and resented being asked.

The mission was a coordinated strike on a mid-level dissident cell. Twenty-eight known members, working in three Quadrants. Names. Photographs. Schedules. Locations. The Council had been watching them for nine months.

The trial was this: each Agema candidate would lead a portion of the strike. Each portion was independent. Each portion was, by design, *interlocking* — meaning that any candidate who wished to could, in theory, sabotage the others without sabotaging the operation.

The candidate who ran the most efficient portion of the strike — *while ensuring no other candidate gained an advantage at their expense* — would be promoted.

Cassius did not look up while he said this.

The trial was, in other words, a betrayal puzzle wrapped around a mass execution.

Sophie was sitting next to me. She squeezed my hand, very briefly, under the table.

It was a friendly squeeze. The kind a friend gives a friend.

It said, *we'll do this together, won't we?*

I squeezed back.

I had not yet decided whether I meant it.

t4.5.text:
The night before the trial, I lay awake.

The thing the body did when it could not sleep was give me the memory I had been trying not to have.

It was a winter afternoon, two years before. We had been Skiritai for three months. We were on a joint patrol that had ended two hours early because nothing had happened. We were walking back along the south wall of the Capitol. The snow had started.

She had stopped to scoop a handful off the railing.

She had thrown it at me without aiming. The snowball had hit me in the side of the neck and gone down inside my collar and I had said something I should not have said in front of a superior officer, and she had laughed — the loud unselfconscious laugh — and I had laughed.

I had laughed for the first time, in this work, in months.

She had been sixteen. The snow had been on her shoulders. The platinum braid had been wet.

She had been, in that one minute, the person who had reminded me — without trying, without knowing she was doing it — that I was still the same age as her, and that being the same age as her had once been a thing that came with laughter.

The memory ended.

I was in my bed in the Capitol. The trial was in the morning.

The body had given me the memory for a reason.

I would have to ignore the reason.

t4.6.text:
The trial began at four in the morning. Three Quadrants. Six candidates. Twenty-eight targets. A Council overseer in a Capitol bunker watching the whole thing on a wall of monitors.

I was running the strike on the second-Quadrant cluster. My subordinate squad had four Skiritai. I had positioned them in a particular cross-fire pattern that I had spent two weeks designing. The pattern depended on a single piece of information staying private until 04:23.

At 04:18, the information was no longer private.

Aren Volk's squad — three Quadrants away — moved in a way that should not have been possible unless they knew the cross-fire pattern. They knew the pattern. They had, in fact, structured their own strike to *use* my pattern as cover, which would, at the moment of commit, make it look — to the Council overseer — like Volk had executed the pattern and I had merely participated.

I had been sabotaged.

I checked the records of who had been near my planning materials in the last week. Three people. Two had not opened the file. The third had opened it twice and been alone in my office for sixteen minutes, two days before, while I was at a Council briefing.

The third was Sophie.

I had four minutes before the operation committed.

choices.label: Betray her back. Feed her coordinates to a third candidate.
Expose her publicly during the debrief.
Win without retaliating. Outperform her cleanly.

t4.7a.text:
I had three minutes.

I sent a single encrypted line to Ilse — the Agema who had the cleanest reputation and the weakest political position. I gave her Sophie's strike coordinates and the timing of Sophie's commit window.

I did not give her any explanation. Ilse was smart enough that no explanation was necessary.

Ilse moved.

At 04:23, when the operations committed, three things happened almost simultaneously. Sophie's strike walked into Ilse's positioning, and Sophie's two best Skiritai went down in friendly crossfire. My pattern executed cleanly because Volk had been watching the wrong window. Volk's portion was, in the bunker monitors, visibly amateurish. Sophie's portion was in chaos.

Sophie was in the chaos.

She did not survive the chaos.

The Council ruled that the operation had been a mixed success, that two candidates had distinguished themselves, and that the seat would go to the candidate whose portion had been *cleanest under contested conditions.*

That was me.

Ilse was reprimanded for *uncoordinated maneuver.* Sophie was buried with full Agema honors. I went to her funeral. I held her father's hand. I told him I was sorry. I had been ready to feel grief. I felt instead the cold satisfaction of a problem solved, and underneath it, smaller, the cold satisfaction of having been *better at this than she had been.*

The second cold satisfaction was the one that kept me up that night.

t4.7b.text:
I let the operation play out. My portion held. Volk's portion, being now badly designed because it had been built around a stolen plan, fell apart in the field. Sophie's portion completed. So did Ilse's.

In the debrief, I spoke last.

I had brought the records. The terminal logs. The file access records. The sixteen-minute window in my office. I projected them onto the wall behind the panel.

Sophie was in the front row. She did not move when she saw the slides. She had known, watching me set up, what I was about to do.

I made the case.

I made it well. I did not raise my voice. I did not make accusations beyond what the evidence supported. I let the panel reach its own conclusion, in the way you let a Council reach its own conclusion when you have already arranged the conclusion to be reachable.

The panel reached the conclusion.

Sophie was reprimanded. Aren Volk was reprimanded by association. Both of them lost the seat.

The seat went to Ilse, who had, by far, run the cleanest portion. The Council noted that I had behaved with admirable restraint and would be *considered favorably* for the next vacancy.

I had, in other words, sacrificed the seat to deny it to Sophie.

Sophie kept her rank. She did not speak to me again. The friendship was over, in a quieter way than it would have been over if she had died. The over-ness was *long.* It would last the rest of our lives, however long they each turned out to be.

t4.7b_aftermath.text:
I stayed Agema for another year.

I worked. I waited. I outmaneuvered three other Agema candidates the slow way — the way Vespera had outmaneuvered her own contemporaries, four decades before.

A year and a half later, the seat opened. I took it.

I was promoted to Hetairoi at nineteen and a half, instead of nineteen.

The half was, in the Council's records, immaterial. It was not immaterial to me.

t4.7c.text:
I had three minutes.

I did nothing.

I did not send a counter-signal. I did not warn Ilse. I did not alter my plan. The plan had been good. The plan had been good before Sophie had stolen part of it. The plan was, even with Volk's misuse of it, good enough that — if I executed my portion *flawlessly* — the operation would still credit me as the lead architect, by the simple fact that I had built the pattern Volk was failing to use.

I committed at 04:23. My portion executed at the speed of light. My Skiritai moved like one body. Three of the six targets were down within two minutes. The other three were down within four. There were no Council casualties on my side.

Volk's side was a mess. Sophie's side completed her objectives but with two casualties and a public-facing disaster — civilians visible in the operational footage. The bunker overseers noted the difference in real time.

The post-trial review was short.

The seat went to me.

Sophie was reprimanded for the sabotage, which she had not denied. The Council required her to acknowledge it formally and to apologize. She did. She apologized in the words she had been given. I accepted in the words I had been given.

We did not speak again.

It was the cleanest path to the seat. It was also a path I could have taken because I had — somehow — kept myself, all the way through, slightly *better* than this place wanted me to be.

I knew, the moment I held it, that *I had not retaliated* could become, very easily, the new lie I told myself.

t4.7d.text:
I let the operation play.

My portion completed adequately. I did not, in fact, even try to maximize. I did the work the way you do work when you have decided, on the way in, that you are not going to fight for the prize.

Sophie's portion completed beautifully. Mine had been the better plan. Hers had been the better *execution,* given that she had stolen mine.

The seat went to her.

I clapped at her promotion. I attended the ceremony. She found me at the bar after. She didn't speak. She took the drink from my hand, drank it, and put it down again.

Then she said, "I'm sorry."

I told her not to be.

I was reassigned, six months later, to a Quadrant administrative post. I oversaw paperwork. I oversaw transit corridor approvals. I oversaw, eventually, my own slow drift out of relevance.

I was alive. I was clean. I was the kind of clean a person becomes when they stop swimming.

It was a kind of life.

I would not, by the time I was twenty-five, recognize the boy in the hallway anymore. He had been swimming.

t4.7e.endingTitle: Ending #7: Executed by the Council (sabotage variant)
t4.7e.text:
I had three minutes.

I used them to break the operation.

I gave my Skiritai a counter-order. *Hold position. Do not fire.* I sent a duplicate of the order, on a channel I knew would be intercepted by Volk's command line and Sophie's command line, *in their names.* All three squads received conflicting orders within the same forty-five seconds. The targets — twenty-eight people in three Quadrants — fled in twenty-eight directions. Sixteen of them got away clean.

The bunker overseers traced the false orders to my terminal in eighty minutes.

I did not run. I sat at my desk and waited.

The Eques who came for me did not say much. They were polite about it. One of them was a woman I had been to a wedding with, two months before. She did not look at me.

The Council did not give me a trial. They gave me a small grey room in the basement of the Capitol. They gave me three days. They asked me, in those three days, several questions, and I gave them several answers, some of them honest.

The end of it was a hallway. I was walked down the hallway. There was a small antechamber. There was a chair.

I had time, in the chair, to think about the boy in the doorway.

He had been small. He had thought there was still a chance the world made sense.

He had, in his way, been right. The chance was small. He had taken it, four years later, and bet on it, and lost.

The losing had been my own. The bet had been honest.

That was the part I would have wanted Bran to know.

It was the part nobody would tell him.

t4.8.text:
The investiture was held in the white room.

The white room is on the top floor of the Hetairoi tower. It is round. The ceiling is high. The walls are bare. It has the acoustics of a chapel, though it is not a chapel. There are no chairs except a single low one in the center. The candidate sits in the chair. The Hetairoi stand in a ring around the candidate. They place a small silver pin on the candidate's collar, one at a time, until all five pins are in place.

I sat in the chair.

The pins went on. Each Hetairoi, in turn, said the small ritual phrase. *Service is privilege. Privilege is service.* The phrase had been said over me five times, now, at five investitures. I had stopped hearing the words years ago.

Vespera was last. Her pin was the smallest. Her hands were warm. She placed it on my collar with a precision I had not expected — she had done this many times, and she had not gotten bored of it.

She leaned in. She said, in a voice only I could hear, "Welcome home."

I rose.

There was a mirror at the back of the room. I had not noticed it, going in. It was not a *prominent* mirror. It was tall and narrow and set into the wall in a way that, from most angles, made it look like a door. I caught my reflection in it as I turned.

I was nineteen.

The face in the mirror was not the face of a nineteen-year-old. It had the right number of years. The wear was wrong. The eyes had four extra years on them, at least, and the mouth had something I had not seen before — a small set to the corners that made the mouth look like it was *deciding* something at all times, even when it was at rest.

I had become the kind of man whose face was always deciding.

The white room had been built precisely so that the candidate would catch this reflection. The mirror was the *point* of the room.

It was the kind of design Vespera would have made.

t5.1.text:
The Council met in a chamber that had no windows.

This was deliberate. Vespera had explained it to me once, in passing — *daylight makes people sentimental, Mika.* The chamber was lit by a soft white light from the ceiling that did not flicker and did not cast shadows. The table was a long polished black. There were six chairs. Five had been occupied for a long time. The sixth had, for two months, been mine.

The first session opened with a small docket.

The fourth item was a sentencing review. A man named Petros Heller. He had posted a printed sheet of paper on a public wall that said *they watch us so we will not see them.* He had been arrested within sixteen hours.

The Hetairoi to my left, Othman, suggested *relocation* as the appropriate sentence. Most relocations were survivable.

He looked around the table for objection.

It was, by ritual, my chance to speak.

The vote would carry whether I spoke or not. The other four had already, with their faces, agreed.

choices.label: Vote against, just to see what happens.

t5.1_with.text:
I said, "I support the recommendation."

It was the first sentence I had ever spoken in the chamber.

Othman nodded. The vote carried, five to nothing. The session moved on.

Vespera — across the table, four chairs down — caught my eye for half a second. The half-smile shifted, briefly, into something that was almost approval.

I noticed the approval. I noticed I had, on the level of the body, *wanted* the approval before I had spoken.

I had wanted it the way Mika at fifteen had wanted Jason's nod in the back of the courtyard. The same hot specific pride.

The pride was not different.

I was. But the pride was not.

t5.1_against.text:
I said, "I oppose the recommendation."

Othman blinked.

He had not been expecting opposition. The other Hetairoi turned to look at me, slowly.

I said, "The relocation is appropriate. I do not object to the outcome. I object to the *unanimity.* I think it is bad practice for this body to rule unanimously on matters of speech. It teaches the public to believe we are a single mind. We are not."

It was the kind of speech a man gives when he has decided to be a slightly visible kind of problem.

Othman thought about it. "You may be right. But you've made the boy's record more complicated for no benefit to him."

The vote carried four to one. I had voted *yes,* with the dissent noted. Petros Heller was relocated.

Vespera caught my eye for half a second.

The half-smile did a different thing. It became more *interested.*

She had, in that one small piece of theater, decided to spend more time on me.

t5.2.text:
My office was in the Hetairoi tower. The window faced east. From it I could see, on a clear day, the wall of the Capitol and beyond it the Quadrants and beyond them the green building on Levant Street where my mother no longer lived.

She had moved a year ago. She had not told me where.

There were three folders on my desk.

The first was a request from Vespera. A handwritten note, three lines. *Tea. My chambers. We have a conversation that is overdue.*

conditionalText.append: The second was Quinn's file. It was still open. Hetairoi clearance now allowed me to do almost anything with it.
The third was a letter from Bran. It had arrived through an old courier route I had not known he knew about. The handwriting was careful. He wanted to see me.

choices.label: Decide what to do with the Quinn file.

t5.3a.text:
I opened the file.

Quinn was thirty pounds lighter than he had been at fifteen. The most recent photograph had been taken six weeks before. They had let his hair grow back. He had grown a small thin beard. He looked, in the photograph, almost peaceful — the peace of a man who had been alone with himself in a small room for a long time and had found, eventually, that the small room was not the worst possible companion.

He was still alive.

He was twenty. He had been in the third-Quadrant facility for five years. He had not been moved. He had not been interrogated since the second year.

I had three options. I had, finally, the rank to use any of them.

choices.label: Close the file. Order his quiet release to a low-priority Quadrant.
Sign the file. End it cleanly.
Leave it open. Let the system decide.

t5.3a_close.text:
I closed it.

I authored the closure myself. I did not delegate. The closure routed Quinn out of the third-Quadrant facility on a low-priority Quadrant transfer, which meant he would be processed quietly, given a new identity card, and reassigned to a labor district somewhere far enough from the Capitol that no one in the Capitol would notice.

I did not give him my own name. I did not write him a letter. I did not, in any way, attach myself to the closure beyond the signature.

He would never know it had been me.

He would simply, one morning, be told he was being released.

I had used Hetairoi privilege, once, for one good thing.

I had paid four years to be able to do it.

I sat at the desk. I noticed the math of the cost.

The math was not flattering. But the math, for the first time in a long time, came out positive on a single line item, and I held the line item carefully, the way I had once held the *clean* feeling of declining Kaden's plan.

I would need it.

t5.3a_sign.text:
I signed.

I did it quickly. I did it without rereading the file. I did it the way I had signed the Marya Fennik order at the Agema desk — without slowing the pen.

The signature went out. The file moved through the system. The execution was scheduled for a week later. It would be, by procedure, quiet.

I went back to the next folder on the desk.

I did not think about it again until that evening, when I was at the bathroom mirror, brushing my teeth. The face in the mirror was the face I had seen at the investiture — the face that was always deciding. I noticed it had not, for the act of signing Quinn's file, done any deciding at all.

The face had simply continued.

The body had, over four years, finished its training.

t5.3a_leave.text:
I closed the file without acting on it.

I did not sign. I did not order release. I did not, in any way, intervene.

I told myself this was a kind of mercy — the system, left alone, would *probably* not act, and Quinn, left alone, would *probably* survive in the facility for several more years.

This was not true. The Council had recently begun *sweeping* old open cases. I knew this because I had sat in on the meeting, two weeks before, where the policy had been announced. Open files older than four years were being closed. Most closures were going one way.

I knew, when I left the file open, what the system would do.

I told myself I had not made a decision. The system would make the decision. The system would carry the weight.

I would notice, six months later, that the system had carried the weight, and that Quinn had been processed in the third batch of the sweep, and that I had not, at any point, picked up a pen.

Not picking up the pen was, in the end, just another way of picking up the pen.

The face in the mirror, that night, did not care about the distinction.

t5.3b.text:
Her chambers had not changed.

She rose. She poured the tea. She had been waiting.

"Sit, Mika."

I sat. We had the tea. The half-smile was on her face.

She said, "Let's not be coy, today. You've come to talk about my retirement."

I had not, in fact, said this on the way over.

She said, "I am sixty-three. I have been on the Council for twenty-two years. I am tired in ways that do not show, yet. I do not intend to be a Council member who is *visibly* tired."

She paused.

"I think it should be you."

She drank.

"It will be a slow handover. Three years. You will continue to learn. I will continue to consult. By the end of three years, you will be the dominant voice in this room, and I will be, formally, retired."

She set down the cup.

"I am offering you what I was offered, and what I took. I do not believe you will refuse. You are, in this respect, very like me."

choices.label: Accept her offer, knowing exactly what it makes you.
Refuse. Tell her you will dismantle the Council.
Don't decide. Let her think you're undecided.

t5.3b_accept_grateful.text:
"I would be honored," I said.

I said it well. I said it the way you say it when you mean it.

I did, in fact, mean it. The mean-ing was perhaps the saddest part of the conversation. I had walked in undecided. The half-smile and the dove-grey flowers and the small careful sentences had collapsed me, in twelve minutes, into the version of myself I had been — privately — trending toward for some time.

She rose. She came around the table. She placed a hand on my shoulder. The hand was very warm.

"Welcome, Mika."

I drank the rest of the tea.

I went back to my office. I sat at my desk. I noticed that the part of me that had four years before stood in a hallway and looked through a door was not, in this office, anywhere I could find. I had looked. The boy was simply not in the room.

I did not, in any precise way, mourn him.

I had, a long time ago, decided not to.

t5.3b_accept_aware.text:
"I accept."

I said it without flourish. I said it without warmth.

I said, "I want to be very clear with you, before I do, that I know exactly what I am accepting. I am accepting the seat. I am accepting it from you. I am accepting that, in three years, I will be sitting where you are now, and I will have, by then, become more like you than I am now. I am accepting that I have, in fact, *become* more like you in each of the last four years, and that this conversation is the last of those years' worth of decisions, made formal."

She looked at me.

The half-smile did not move.

"Yes. That is exactly what you are accepting."

"I want you to know I know."

"I know you know. That is part of why I am offering."

She poured me more tea.

"Most men who climb to this seat arrive without knowing. They are surprised, after, by what they have become. The surprise is, in itself, an excuse. The surprise lets them believe they are *not* the seat. They are. They simply did not look directly at it on the way up."

She drank.

"You looked. It is a small distinction. But I find it interesting. You will be a slightly different kind of Council head than I have been. I am curious to see how you fail."

t5.3b_refuse.text:
"No," I said.

She blinked.

"I am going to dismantle the Council," I said. "I came here to tell you. I owed you that much."

She set down her cup.

She did not laugh. She did not get angry. She did not try to talk me out of it. She did, however, look — for one half-second — *disappointed.* The disappointment was for *me.* As if I had, by refusing, become a smaller version of the thing she had been considering for years.

"I see."

She picked up the cup again.

"Well, Mika. I will not retire, then. I will, in fact, become very interested in the next several decisions you make. I do not threaten. I observe."

She smiled.

"And I outlast."

I left the chambers.

The conversation had, in some way, been the easiest of the four years. I had told the truth. The truth would compound, daily, for as long as either of us was alive.

I had refused without a plan.

I would have to develop a plan.

I had, perhaps, three months.

t5.3b_delay.text:
"I want to think."

She nodded.

"Think."

"I'd like a few months."

"You may have them."

She said it without any irritation. She refilled my cup. We talked, for the rest of the visit, about lighter things.

I left without committing.

I noticed, walking back, that *not committing* was a form of commitment. She had read it as such. She would now, for the duration of the few months I had asked for, be making her own arrangements — building her own contingencies, identifying my potential weaknesses, preparing the pieces she would need to make sure that, when I finally answered, I answered the way she had wanted me to.

I had, by asking for time, given her time also.

I had not understood, going in, that this was the symmetric cost.

I would, soon enough, decide.

I had, by then, fewer real options than I had thought I had walking in.

t5.3c.text:
I met him at the cafe he had named in the letter.

It was a small place, three blocks from the Capitol wall on the inside, the kind of cafe Eques went to when they did not want to be seen by other Eques.

He was sitting at a corner table. He had a coffee in front of him. He had grown again. He was sixteen now — a year younger than I had been when I had killed Jason — and he was, by some accounting, almost a man. There was a small scar on his lower lip that was new.

He stood when I came in. He did not hug me. He shook my hand. The handshake was firm.

He said, "Hello, Mika."

He said it the way you say *hello* to a man who is, in some ways, a stranger.

We sat. He had ordered me a coffee. He had remembered how I took it.

He said, "I want to know what you've done."

conditionalText.append: *He said it without preamble because, of course, he had been waiting to say it for four years.*

choices.label: Warn him. Leave the city. Change his name. Hide.
Bring him into the Capitol — protected.
Cut him off. For his safety, never see him again.
Tell him the truth. Beg his forgiveness.

t5.3c_warn.text:
I told him to leave.

I told him I had access, now, to documents he would not survive being on. I told him there were lists. I told him there was a sweep coming, within months. I told him to change his name and disappear into one of the smaller Quadrants and that I had a contact who could move him quietly.

He listened. He did not interrupt. He did not weep.

When I was done he said, "Will you come with me?"

"No."

"Why not?"

"Because I'm finishing something."

He nodded.

He drank the rest of his coffee. He stood. He shook my hand again.

"Goodbye, Mika."

He said it like he meant *for the rest of our lives.*

t5.3c_protect.text:
I told him to come to the Capitol.

I told him I had quarters that could fit him. I told him I could place him in a school inside the wall — a real one, the kind aristocratic children went to — and he would be on the rolls of *citizens of the Capitol,* which meant the Council would no longer be able to act against him without consulting me.

He listened.

He drank his coffee. He looked at me with an expression I could not place.

"I'm not coming."

"Bran."

"If I come into the Capitol, I am inside the wall, and I am yours. I am not yours, Mika. I have not been yours for a long time. You know this."

He drank.

"But thank you."

He said it without sarcasm. He thanked me, sincerely, for offering — for offering *the kind of thing I could offer.*

He stood.

"I'll write," he said. "Sometimes. Not from here."

He left.

I had bought him, by offering, a kind of polite distance I had not had before.

t5.3c_cut.text:
I told him I could not see him again.

I told him it was for his safety. I told him that the closer he was to me — physically, by association, by *anyone in the system noticing the connection* — the more likely it was that someday his name would land on a list I could not pull it off of in time.

He listened.

He waited a long time before answering.

Then: "Okay."

He said it the way he had said *okay* in the kitchen four years before.

He stood. He did not shake my hand this time.

"Mom asks about you. I tell her you're fine."

He left.

I sat at the table.

I noticed I had used the language of *protection* to do something else. I noticed that *protection,* in this conversation, had been a word for *making my life less complicated.*

t5.3c_truth.text:
I told him.

All of it. The library hallway. The file. The Eques in the doorway. The morale report. The runner at the checkpoint. Ennis Ardin's family. Marya Fennik. Jason in his office at midnight. Sophie at the trial. Kaden in the apartment. The signed kill orders. Vespera offering me her seat. The full account. In one cup of coffee.

He listened.

When I was done he was crying, very quietly. He was not crying loudly. He had learned, somewhere, that loud was dangerous.

He said, "Why are you telling me this now?"

"Because you asked."

"You could have lied. You're good at it."

"I am."

"So why."

I thought about it.

"Because I have not, in four years, said the truth to anyone who had a right to hear it. And you have a right to hear it. And it's the only thing left I can give you."

He nodded. He stood.

"I won't tell anyone. I won't write again. I won't visit. I will, for the rest of my life, know who you are. I think I will have to live alone with knowing. I don't know yet whether I forgive you. I don't think I will."

He paused.

"But I love you. I wanted you to know that, before I leave."

He left.

For the eleven minutes it took me to walk back to the Capitol, I had been a person again.

t5.4.text:
Vespera proposed it on a Tuesday.

It was on the docket as *Resolution 412: Extension of Civilian Monitoring Protocols to Eques Quadrant Personnel.* She presented it, briefly. The argument was about *fairness.* In a system that watched everyone else, why should Eques be exempt? The exemption, she said, had originated as a matter of operational security — but operational security, in the modern Council, was robust enough that the exemption was no longer necessary, and the exemption was, increasingly, *philosophically uncomfortable.*

The other four Hetairoi nodded along. They had been briefed.

Except — by the rules of the chamber — the vote required a unanimity, or it had to be tabled for a public review. The other four would vote yes. The vote turned, technically, on me.

Vespera looked at me across the table.

She did not smile. She did not need to.

She had timed this proposal for the week after I had spoken with her — a test, and an acceleration. A way of consolidating her own power by removing the only branch of society she could not fully see.

choices.label: Vote yes — the Eques should not be above the system.
Vote no — the Eques privilege is the only space reform can breathe in.

t5.4_yes.text:
"Yes."

The word was small. It carried.

The vote went through five to nothing. Resolution 412 passed. The Eques would, within ninety days, begin to be brought under the same monitoring infrastructure as the rest of the city.

Vespera nodded at me. The half-smile was on her face.

I had, with one word, done the thing I had — at fifteen — sworn to come into this building specifically to *prevent.*

The symmetry was clean. It was the kind of clean a person could write a poem about, if a person were the kind of person who wrote poems.

I was not.

t5.4_no.text:
"No."

The chamber was very quiet.

It was the first time, in my term, that I had broken unanimity on a major resolution. The other four turned to look at me.

Vespera did not look surprised.

I said, "I believe the exemption is the *only space* in this system that allows the system to consider itself. If the Eques are watched the way the Quadrants are watched, the Council loses the only branch of society capable of independent reflection. I will not vote to take that away."

It was the cleanest sentence I had spoken in four years.

The vote was tabled. The resolution went to public review. It would return to the Council in six weeks. By then the political cost of my dissent would have done its work.

Vespera did not look at me.

I was, formally, in opposition now.

t5.4_abstain.text:
"I abstain."

By the rules of the chamber, an abstention had the same procedural effect as a *no.*

But abstention had a different *flavor* than dissent. It told the room that I did not have a position. That I was — in this matter — not a colleague but a fence-sitter. Vespera disliked fence-sitters. They were, she had once told me, *unfinished people.*

The vote was tabled.

She did not look at me with hostility.

She looked at me with something more troubling: *curiosity.* As if, by abstaining, I had not lost her — but I had, in some way, become less interesting. I had given her less to work with.

I had, by trying to keep my options open, narrowed them.

t5.5.text:
It was three in the morning.

I was alone in my Hetairoi office. The window was dark. The Capitol was quiet.

The body did, again, the thing it does when I will not let it sleep.

It gave me the hallway.

I was fifteen. I was walking the long way. The door was half-open. Quinn's face was on the screen. The Eques was at the desk.

I had remembered this scene in many forms by now. From inside myself. From above. From the boy's perspective. From the Eques's.

This time, I let the camera settle on the Eques.

He turned his head. He saw me in the doorway. He stood. He came to the door. He looked at me.

He had pale skin and dark brown hair, kept short and neat. He had sharp brown eyes with a slight downturn that gave him a permanent half-tired, half-watchful look. He had a small scar on his left jaw. He was wearing the white-and-gold of a Hetairoi.

He was, in the memory, nineteen.

He was me.

He looked at the boy in the doorway. The boy looked back. There was a small silence in the hall — the kind of silence that happens when two people who are the same person, separated by four years and one signature, recognize each other.

The Hetairoi said, very gently, "I'm sorry. Come with me."

The boy nodded.

He came. He came because he had no other option. He came because the Hetairoi had been kind.

I watched myself walk down the hallway with myself.

I watched the boy disappear around the corner.

I did not follow.

I sat at the Hetairoi desk in the present, alone, in the dark, and I noticed that the door I had walked through, four years ago, was a door I had myself — at this older age, in this office — held open.

The whole architecture made sense.

I was, in this place, both the boy and the Eques. I had been both, all along.

t5.6.text:
Dawn.

I had been at the desk for hours.

The decisions were lined up in front of me the way the dossiers had lined up at the Agema desk a year before. Each one a folder. Each one a person. Each one a future.

I could move on Vespera. I had the intelligence. I had the timing. I had the alliances I had — quietly, slowly — built in the corners she had not been watching. The coup was *possible.* The coup was even, with effort, *probable.*

I could accept her offer. Become the heir. Inherit the seat in three years and, after that, run this Council the way she had run it, with my own modest variations on the work.

I could propose my own reform. Submit, this morning, a structural revision of the Council's surveillance architecture. The proposal had been written. It was in a drawer. It had been written for months.

I could resist. Stand on the floor of the chamber, this afternoon, and refuse a key vote in a way that could not be quietly undone. They would have to remove me. They would.

Or I could stop.

Get up from the desk. Walk away. Disappear into the small room of myself that had, perhaps, been waiting for four years. Refuse to continue being the man who sat in this chair.

There was a mirror on the wall opposite the desk. The face in the mirror was nineteen. The face in the mirror was always deciding.

The face had to decide.

choices.label: Move against Vespera. Take the Council.
Accept her succession. Become her heir.
Propose your own reform. Build a new Council.
Resist a vote publicly. Force their hand.
You can't continue. Step away from the desk.
